EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Tuesday April 29, a cold front crossed the region causing showers and thunderstorms to develop. Conditions and timing were favorable ahead of the front as it approached the Buffalo forecast area with CAPE values of 1,000+ J/kg and bulk shear values of 40+ kts. Numerous reports of wind damage and severe hail were received for areas south and southeast of Lake Ontario.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The National Weather Service conducted a storm survey to examine damage in the Town of Phelps in Ontario County from April 29, 2025. Extensive hardwood tree damage was evident along McBurney Road just to the north of the New York State Thruway approximately two miles west of State Route 88. Tree damage continued down McBurney Road until about half of a mile before Falkey Road. Tree damage was evident along both sides of Stryker Road to the north of McBurney Road with multiple hardwood trees losing numerous large branches. In addition, there was extensive structural damage to multiple buildings on a farm at the corner of McBurney Road and Stryker Road. This included a large barn that was fully shifted off of its foundation. Another barn lost the majority of its metal roof. An old farm house adjacent to one of the barns experienced total structural collapse of the concrete block walls on two sides. ||Further up Stryker Road, a residence experienced the partial loss of roof flashing material and the total loss of a garage. Debris from the garage and roof were strewn across Stryker Road to the east-northeast of the former structure and into the trees about one-quarter of a mile away. ||Tree damage continued to the north and west along Stryker Road and along County Road 25. This included the uprooting of multiple hardwood trees in a mobile home park. Two of these very large trees fell onto residences in the mobile home park.||Based upon the lack of a concentrated damage path within a large swath of damage and by virtue of the fact the damage area was less than 2.5 miles in diameter, the National Weather Service storm survey team determined the damage was a result of straight line winds associated with a microburst.||Peak winds were estimated at 95 mph, the damage path length was 1.5 miles long and 1,850 yards wide. One injury was reported with this damage from the thunderstorms.

An elongated area of low pressure moved across NY and New England during New Year's Day intensifying into a strong area of low pressure that stalled across Newfoundland on January 2nd and 3rd.||Initially there was a mix of snow and rain across northern NY on New Year's morning with accumulating snow during the afternoon into midday of January 2nd. A widespread 3 to 6 inches fell in the Adirondacks with a trace to few inches in the St. Lawrence and Champlain valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A general 3 to 6 inches of snow fell across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large stationary storm in Newfoundland delivered an favorable flow of moisture and energy southwestward into northern NY and VT on January 7th-9th.||Snow overspread northern sections and was enhanced by the favorable NNW upslope of the Adirondacks and Green Mountains beginning on the morning of January 7th and continued intermittently until ending on January 9th.||Snowfall totals ranged from a dusting to 2 inches in the Champlain Valley of NY with 3 to 6 inches in the eastern Adirondacks and St. Lawrence River Valley and 8 to 16+ inches in the western Adirondacks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall ranged from 3 to 8 inches across the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On March 16, a strong area of low pressure tracked across the Central Great Lakes into southeast Ontario and southwest Quebec. The warm front associated with the system tracked north across the western New York area during the morning hours and an associated low level jet increased winds out of the south, especially for downslope areas near Lake Erie. This resulted in non thunderstorm winds that caused damage across much of Chautauqua County prior to the arrival of severe thunderstorms during the early afternoon. Wind Advisories or Warnings were in effect for all of the western and north central New York counties due to the potential for gusty winds. Convection developed across Ontario and Pennsylvania during the late morning hours along a pre-frontal trough associated with the system. As the pre-frontal trough and early convection tracked into and then across the western New York area, conditions were favorable for strong to severe thunderstorms to develop. Severe thunderstorms caused damage across much of the Buffalo forecast area, mainly inland from Lake Erie and extending to the North Country. ||In addition to the damage noted above across Chautauqua County, a measured wind gust of 66 MPH occurred at the Dunkirk Airport during the morning of March 16.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees and wires downed in the Town of Sodus.

Convection developed across Ontario and Pennsylvania during the late morning hours along a pre-frontal trough associated with the system. Severe thunderstorms caused damage across much of the Buffalo forecast area, mainly inland from Lake Erie and extending to the North Country. ||In addition to the damage noted above across Chautauqua County, a measured wind gust of 66 MPH occurred at the Dunkirk Airport during the morning of March 16.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure was located across Hudson and James Bay on January 27th while a cold front from this system approached northern NY. Strong WSW wind gusts of 45 to 60+ mph accompanied the front across northern NY which led to scattered power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous wind gusts in excess of 50 mph were observed, including a wind gust of 62 mph in Malone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak wave of low pressure developed and traveled along a stationary front draped across northern NY and VT during the evening of March 28th and continuing into the 29th.  Snowfall amounts ranged  from several inches across southern portions of northern NY to a half foot or more in the northern sections along the Canadian border. Meanwhile across the southern tier there was mixed winter precipitation, including freezing rain and icing that accumulated upwards of an half inch in some locations. Power outages across northern NY were around 5000.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals across the area were 5 to 10 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a brief lull, an intense band of lake effect snow with a multi-lake connection moved back south into Madison and Onondaga Counties during the early morning hours on Tuesday, the 18th. The lake effect snow continued to fall Tuesday night into Wednesday morning before dissipating and coming to an end as high pressure moved in. The heavy lake effect snow combined with the strong winds led to near zero visibility at times. Snowfall rates in the intense band reached 2 to 4 inches/hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake effect snow accumulated between 6 and 10 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moving across the area during the evening hours of the 16th initially led to some light snow and a quick drop in temperatures, rapidly freezing any wet or untreated surfaces. Very strong, locally damaging winds developed in the wake of the front resulting in wind advisories and high wind warnings to be in place through the evening of Monday, the 17th. One location reported a wind gust greater than 58 mph during this time. With the much colder air streaming in across the Great Lakes, lake effect snow began to develop later in the evening of the 16th and then became more organized and intense on the 17th as a multi-lake connection developed. ||The intense lake effect snow band lifted north during the afternoon of the 17th and continued to bring heavy snow to Oneida County through the night of the 18th, while Onondaga and Madison Counties got a brief reprieve from the steadiest lake effect snow. The heavy lake effect snow combined with the strong winds led to near zero visibility and blizzard-like conditions. Snowfall rates in the intense band reached 2 to 4 inches/hour. Additional snow showers continued into early Wednesday before coming to an end as high pressure moved in. This prolonged lake effect snow event combined with previous rounds of lake effect snow led to the damage of or collapse of dozens of structures, mostly across northern Oneida County as the culmination of the weight of the heavy, wet snow became too much. With some of the structures being barns, there was the loss of livestock as well. Also, the collapse of a sunroom roof in Rome, NY resulted in serious injuries to the homeowner when it fell on him.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The New York State Mesonet station at Roxbury measured a wind gust of 64 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A departing upper level disturbance led to the development of flow from the west-northwest off of Lake Ontario and resulting lake effect snowfall for mainly Oneida County. Lake effect snow started during the evening of Sunday, the 9th, and continued into Monday before lifting north and tapering off Monday evening. Snowfall rates reached 1 to 2 inches per hour Sunday evening when a multi-lake connection developed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake effect snow accumulated between 3 and 9 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strengthening storm system over the central Appalachians tracked northeastward and across the area on the afternoon of Sunday, the 16th. Snow developed ahead of the surface low, getting underway during the late morning and afternoon on the 15th. Warmer air aloft moved in much faster than projected and a change over to sleet or a wintry mix started the evening of the 15th. As the main low tracked toward the area, a change to freezing rain then plain rain occurred on the 16th as warmer air continued to push north. Although snow and ice totals underperformed as a whole, the additional wintry precipitation on top of heavy lake effect snow that had fallen prior did lead to the collapse of a fire department pavilion and the partial collapse of a local metal fabrication business building.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated between 1 and 4 inches. Total ice accumulation was up to 0.2 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moving across the area during the evening hours of the 16th initially led to some light snow and a quick drop in temperatures, rapidly freezing any wet or untreated surfaces. Very strong, locally damaging winds developed in the wake of the front resulting in wind advisories and high wind warnings to be in place through the evening of Monday, the 17th. One location reported a wind gust greater than 58 mph during this time. With the much colder air streaming in across the Great Lakes, lake effect snow began to develop later in the evening of the 16th and then became more organized and intense on the 17th as a multi-lake connection developed. ||The intense lake effect snow band lifted north during the afternoon of the 17th and continued to bring heavy snow to Oneida County through the night of the 18th, while Onondaga and Madison Counties got a brief reprieve from the steadiest lake effect snow. The heavy lake effect snow combined with the strong winds led to near zero visibility and blizzard-like conditions. Snowfall rates in the intense band reached 2 to 4 inches/hour. Additional snow showers continued into early Wednesday before coming to an end as high pressure moved in. This prolonged lake effect snow event combined with previous rounds of lake effect snow led to the damage of or collapse of dozens of structures, mostly across northern Oneida County as the culmination of the weight of the heavy, wet snow became too much. With some of the structures being barns, there was the loss of livestock as well. Also, the collapse of a sunroom roof in Rome, NY resulted in serious injuries to the homeowner when it fell on him.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake effect snow accumulated between 6 and 14 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A developing surface low moved from the Ohio Valley across central NY during the evening hours of Saturday, the 8th, which brought a 3 to 8 hour period of snow and even a wintry mix to parts of the Southern Tier as temperatures aloft rose to slightly above freezing. This was a fast-moving system aided by a strong upper level jet that led to a quick arrival and departure of this feature. Even though this was a quick-moving system, there was a focused midlevel front that led to 1 to 2 inch per hour snowfall rates in the evening with the heaviest snow falling across Oneida County. Snow continued to fall overnight and lifted north of the area during the early morning hours of the 9th. As the storm system pushed off to the north and west, wrap around moisture and northwest flow led to some lingering lake effect snow showers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated between 4 and 8 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strengthening storm system over the central Appalachians tracked northeastward and across the area on the afternoon of Sunday, the 16th. Snow developed ahead of the surface low, getting underway during the late morning and afternoon on the 15th. Warmer air aloft moved in much faster than projected and a change over to sleet or a wintry mix started the evening of the 15th. As the main low tracked toward the area, a change to freezing rain then plain rain occurred on the 16th as warmer air continued to push north. Although snow and ice totals underperformed as a whole, the additional wintry precipitation on top of heavy lake effect snow that had fallen prior did lead to the collapse of a fire department pavilion and the partial collapse of a local metal fabrication business building.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated between 1 and 4 inches. Total ice accumulation was up to 0.15 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front crossed the area during the afternoon of Thursday, the 13th, and a strong flow from the west-northwest occurred in its wake. Lake effect snow developed downwind of Lake Ontario Thursday night and continued into Friday before starting to lift off to the north Friday afternoon and evening. With a multi-lake connection developing and mostly uniform winds at the lower levels late Thursday evening into early Friday, heavy snow fell at times over Oneida, Madison and Onondaga counties. Winds gusted between 30 and 45 mph at times leading to areas of blowing and drifting snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake effect snow accumulated between 4 and 23 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to locally damaging winds developed across eastern New York in the wake of a cold front beginning in the afternoon hours on March 6, 2025 and continued into March 7, 2025. Widespread wind gusts between 40 and 60 mph were observed with some locally higher gusts including a 69 mph wind gust at the Schenectady County Airport. These winds resulted in some downed trees and wires across the area. Strong winds continued on March 8 but were not as strong with some peak gusts between 40 and 50 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 43 mph wind gust was recorded at the New York State Mesonet site near Copake.

Snowfall rates reached 1 to 2 inches per hour Sunday evening when a multi-lake connection developed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake effect snow accumulated between 7 and 15 inches.

Cold air filtering into the region behind a passing surface low set the stage for a lake effect snow event east-southeast of the lakes. While snow was heavy off of both lakes, significant snowfall amounts off of Lake Ontario were particularly heavy with reports of over five feet of accumulation. With cold air moving into the region, a persistent broad scale troughing pattern set up over the area from New Years day through the weekend. 850 hPa temperatures cooled to as low as -15 degrees Celsius during the event while lake water temperatures were around +4 degrees Celsius. ||Notable reported snowfall amounts include: 65.0 inches in Williamstown, 32.3 inches in Cleveland, 29.1 inches in Mexico, 26. 4 inches in Little Valley, 26.3 inches in Cattaraugus, 23.5 inches in Constableville, 21.1 inches in Mayville and Dewittville, 18.5 inches in Arcade, 17.5 inches in Hooker, 16.5 inches in Springville, 8.0 inches in Sterling.

Lake effect snow developed east of Lake Ontario in the wake of a weak passing warm frontal boundary that resulted in earlier synoptic snow across the area. Passing cold fronts along with a trough over the region continued to support lake effect snow off of Lake Ontario through the early morning of January 27th. Off of Lake Ontario, much higher equilibrium heights compared to Lake Erie allowed for organized lake snow to continue while off of Lake Erie, little to no lake snow developed. An upstream connection with the Georgian Bay and Lake Huron was also a contributing factor to the lake effect event off the east and southeast end of Lake Ontario. Marginally cold temperatures at the surface and aloft limited the heavier snow amounts to the higher terrain. ||Snowfall amounts for this event include: 12.5 inches in Castorland, 12.0 inches in Taylorville and Lorraine, 11.2 inches in Hooker, 11.0 inches in Copenhagen and Croghan and 10.5 inches in Barnes Corners.

A powerful area of low pressure tracked across northern Ontario and Quebec on January 27th and 28th. The strong cold front associated with the system tracked south across the western and north central New York area, causing moderate to heavy snowfall at times during the night of January 27th into the morning hours of January 28th. Lake enhancement and upsloping added to the snowfall amounts, especially northeast of Lake Ontario where up to a foot of snow accumulated. Because of the strength of the area of low pressure, dropping to as low as 966 hPa, a tight pressure gradient was present over the region.||Snowfall amounts for this event include: 12.0 inches in Lowville, 11.2 inches in Copenhagen, 10.2 inches in Castorland, 10.0 inches in Constableville and 9.0 inches in Calcium. ||Notable wind gusts for this event include: 66 mph at the Batavia New York State Mesonet, 63 mph at the Buffalo Airport, 61 mph at the Niagara Falls Airport, 60 mph at Dunkirk Airport and at the Fredonia C-Man station and 58 mph at the York New York State Mesonet.

A quick moving clipper system tracking directly across northern New York caused moderate to heavy snowfall across portions of the Buffalo forecast area. This was especially the case for areas east of Lake Ontario where closer proximity to the passing surface low, combined with lake enhancement and terrain influences caused over a foot of snow to accumulate in some areas. While snow was widespread across the entire Buffalo forecast area, the greatest amounts occurred over Lewis County. ||Snowfall amounts include: 16.5 inches in Constableville, 14.2 inches in Highmarket, 10.0 inches in Lowville and 9.0 inches in Chases Lake.

A lake effect band of snow developed behind a departing area of low pressure late on February 9th. A west to west-northwest flow favored areas across Oswego and Northern Cayuga counties for the heaviest snow amounts. Temperatures aloft at 850 hPa cooled to -15 degrees Celsius as a trough was in place over the region. This combined with synoptic moisture and the long fetch over Lake Ontario resulted in heavy lake effect snow for areas east to southeast of the lake. ||Snowfall amounts for this event include: 27.9 inches in Palermo, 22.1 inches in Minetto and 12.0 inches in Sterling.

Similar to earlier in the week, another band of lake effect snow set up off of Lake Ontario, focusing on areas east to southeast of the lake. Again, behind a passing area of low pressure, cooling temperatures aloft at 850 hPa along with a trough over the region and optimal flow over Lake Ontario resulted in heavy snowfall for areas that received heavy snow earlier in the week. After this lake effect event was over, some areas received nearly 60 inches of snow for the week between the two events. Heavy snowfall for the week, combined with subsequent snow and rainfall caused roof damage to some structures in Oswego County, including numerous structures where entire roofs collapsed due to the weight of the snow and water equivalent. ||Snowfall amounts from this event includes: 35.8 inches in Palermo and 31.0 inches in Cleveland.

Behind the passing winter storm from the previous few days, a favorable lake effect setup developed off of Lake Ontario. A lingering trough and synoptic moisture, along with cold temperatures aloft resulted in widespread lake effect snow off of much of Lake Ontario from Orleans County eastward. The heaviest snow fell for areas east to southeast of Lake Ontario, where a better upstream fetch over the lake allowed for better organization of a lake snow band. ||Snowfall amounts for this event include: 42.2 inches in Mexico, 20.6 inches in Minetto, 16.8 inches in Highmarket, 14.0 inches in Constableville, 8.5 inches in Newark and 7.4 inches in Medina.

A long duration lake effect snow event brought significant snowfall, especially near and north of the NYS Thruway corridor. Snow started on Wednesday, the 1st, and then increased in intensity on Thursday, the 2nd, through midday Friday, the 3rd, as a multi-lake connection set up. A multi-lake connection returned at times through the weekend and moisture from the Atlantic was drawn into the region along the backside of a broad upper low over eastern Canada enhancing the lake effect. The main lake effect band fluctuated north and south at times and brought locally heavy bouts of snow several times across portions of central NY through the 5th before finally dissipating. The band resided the longest over northern Oneida County, where snow amounts as high as 65-75 inches were reported. Strong winds accompanied the lake effect snow at times leading to blowing and drifting snow and low visibility.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake effect snow accumulated between 15 and 48 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long duration lake effect snow event brought significant snowfall, especially near and north of the NYS Thruway corridor. Snow started on Wednesday, the 1st, and then increased in intensity on Thursday, the 2nd, through midday Friday, the 3rd, as a multi-lake connection set up. A multi-lake connection returned at times through the weekend and moisture from the Atlantic was drawn into the region along the backside of a broad upper low over eastern Canada enhancing the lake effect. The main lake effect band fluctuated north and south at times and brought locally heavy bouts of snow several times across portions of central NY through the 5th before finally dissipating. The band resided the longest over northern Oneida County, where snow amounts as high as 65-75 inches were reported. Strong winds accompanied the lake effect snow at times leading to blowing and drifting snow and low visibility.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake effect snow accumulated between 7 and 17 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lake effect snow developed east of Lake Ontario in the wake of a weak passing warm frontal boundary that resulted in earlier synoptic snow across the area. Passing cold fronts along with a trough over the region continued to support lake effect snow off of Lake Ontario through the early morning of January 27th. Off of Lake Ontario, much higher equilibrium heights compared to Lake Erie allowed for organized lake snow to continue while off of Lake Erie, little to no lake snow developed. An upstream connection with the Georgian Bay and Lake Huron was also a contributing factor to the lake effect event off the east and southeast end of Lake Ontario. Marginally cold temperatures at the surface and aloft limited the heavier snow amounts to the higher terrain. ||Snowfall amounts for this event include: 12.5 inches in Castorland, 12.0 inches in Taylorville and Lorraine, 11.2 inches in Hooker, 11.0 inches in Copenhagen and Croghan and 10.5 inches in Barnes Corners.

Lake effect snow off of Lake Ontario drifted south across Oneida County on Tuesday, the 14th. Snow was heavy at times with snowfall rates up to 2 to 3 inches per hour in the most intense band of snow. The lake effect snow moved south Tuesday night as winds became more northwesterly and moved into portions of Onondaga and Madison Counties. Winds shifted back to the west during the day Wednesday, the 15th, which allowed the lake effect snow to lift back north through the NYS Thruway corridor and into Oneida County. The snow dissipated as it lifted north out of the area Wednesday night as upper level ridging built in aloft.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake effect snow accumulated between 7 and 17 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Behind the passing winter storm from the previous few days, a favorable lake effect setup developed off of Lake Ontario. A lingering trough and synoptic moisture, along with cold temperatures aloft resulted in widespread lake effect snow off of much of Lake Ontario from Orleans County eastward. The heaviest snow fell for areas east to southeast of Lake Ontario, where a better upstream fetch over the lake allowed for better organization of a lake snow band. ||Snowfall amounts for this event include: 42.2 inches in Mexico, 20.6 inches in Minetto, 16.8 inches in Highmarket, 14.0 inches in Constableville, 8.5 inches in Newark and 7.4 inches in Medina.

A clipper system with limited moisture moved through early on Sunday, the 26th, and winds became westerly in its wake leading to the development of lake effect snow off of Lake Ontario. This lake effect activity drifted south into northern Oneida County during the morning hours and continued until the early evening when it drifted back to the north and out of the county. The main band did overperform during the morning hours prompting a Winter Weather Advisory to be upgraded to a Winter Storm Warning for lake effect snow. Some gusty winds accompanied the lake effect snow band as well leading to blowing snow and reduced visibility.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake effect snow accumulated between 4 and 9 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front crossed through eastern New York between late Monday, January 27 through the early morning of Tuesday, January 28, 2025. As a result, snow showers spread into the region, bringing light to locally moderate accumulations to the higher elevations of the Southwest Adirondacks in northern Herkimer County and snow squalls to areas farther south and east. Strong winds across portions of eastern New York resulted in occasional gusts between 35 and 50 mph. Snow squalls, with brief bursts of moderate to locally heavy snowfall rates, reduced visibility to less than a quarter of a mile in places, producing localized wind gusts on the order of 30 to 40 mph, and led to light accumulations of snow between the hours of 5 and 8 AM. Some accidents were reported on the morning on Tuesday, January 28 which led to travel delays during the morning commute. ||Key impacts: slippery travel conditions, car accidents, travel delays.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow Squall. A local highway webcam showed less than one quarter of a mile visibility with a nearby wind gust of 32 mph south of Westerlo.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold air filtering into the region behind a passing surface low set the stage for a lake effect snow event east-southeast of the lakes. While snow was heavy off of both lakes, significant snowfall amounts off of Lake Ontario were particularly heavy with reports of over five feet of accumulation. With cold air moving into the region, a persistent broad scale troughing pattern set up over the area from New Years day through the weekend. 850 hPa temperatures cooled to as low as -15 degrees Celsius during the event while lake water temperatures were around +4 degrees Celsius. ||Notable reported snowfall amounts include: 65.0 inches in Williamstown, 32.3 inches in Cleveland, 29.1 inches in Mexico, 26. 4 inches in Little Valley, 26.3 inches in Cattaraugus, 23.5 inches in Constableville, 21.1 inches in Mayville and Dewittville, 18.5 inches in Arcade, 17.5 inches in Hooker, 16.5 inches in Springville, 8.0 inches in Sterling.

Another clipper system moved across the region during the evening hours on January 28 through the early morning hours on January 30, 2025. Moderate to locally heavy snowfall occurred across much of the Mohawk Valley and Adirondacks where snowfall totals between 5 and 12 inches occurred. Lesser snow accumulations fell elsewhere. Strong winds developed once again on January 29 with widespread gusts between 40 and 55 mph. In addition, snow showers and localized snow squalls led to brief whiteout conditions, moderate to heavy snowfall rates and gusty winds.||Key impacts: travel delays.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow squall. Visibility reduced to one quarter of a mile seen on the New York State Thruway cameras near the interchange of Interstate 890 and Interstate 90 near Scotia.

Eastern New York was impacted by an arctic airmass from January 21-22, 2025. The combination of below normal air temperatures, breezy winds, and nighttime radiational cooling led to minimum wind chill (or feels'-like temperatures) between -10 and -30 degrees Fahrenheit with the more widespread readings occurring in the early morning hours on January 22. The lowest of these air temperatures and wind chill values occurred in the highest terrain areas of the Southwest Adirondacks, Eastern Catskills, Helderbergs, and Taconics.

A clipper low pressure system brought scattered, light snow showers to portions of eastern New York, mainly north and west of Albany, on the afternoon of Saturday, January 25. A west-east band of lake effect snow developed in the wake of a passing cold front on January 13, 2025 and continued into January 14 before dropping southward and weakening. Eastern New York was impacted by an arctic airmass from January 21-22, 2025. The combination of below normal air temperatures, breezy winds, and nighttime radiational cooling led to minimum wind chill (or feels'-like temperatures) between -10 and -30 degrees Fahrenheit with the more widespread readings occurring in the early morning hours on January 22. The lowest of these air temperatures and wind chill values occurred in the highest terrain areas of the Southwest Adirondacks, Eastern Catskills, Helderbergs, and Taconics.

Another clipper system moved across the region during the evening hours on January 28 through the early morning hours on January 30, 2025. Moderate to locally heavy snowfall occurred across much of the Mohawk Valley and Adirondacks where snowfall totals between 5 and 12 inches occurred. Lesser snow accumulations fell elsewhere. Strong winds developed once again on January 29 with widespread gusts between 40 and 55 mph. In addition, snow showers and localized snow squalls led to brief whiteout conditions, moderate to heavy snowfall rates and gusty winds.||Key impacts: travel delays.

An arctic airmass over the Lower Great Lakes developed a lake aggregate surface trough at the start of the event, which resulted in the development of a mesolow over the Niagara Frontier late on the night of the 19th. Heavy snow with the mesolow stretched as far east as the Rochester Metro area. A deep mid and upper level trough and re-enforcing cold air continued the favorable conditions for lake effect snow to continue into the middle of the week. 850 hPa temperatures dropping to near -25 degrees Celsius resulted in plenty of instability over the lakes and combined with the passing troughs and west to west-southwest flow, areas east to east-northeast of the lakes received heavy snowfall during this event. Areas east of Lake Erie received over 18 inches of snow, while areas east of Lake Ontario picked up over three feet of snow. This was a more widespread event than the typical lake effect event with many more counties impacted and with warnings in effect compared to normal. ||Snowfall amounts for this event include: 41 inches in Hooker, 40.3 inches in Adams, 40 inches in Barnes Corners, 39.2 inches in Henderson Harbor, 27.4 inches in Watertown, 22 inches in Eden, 21 inches in East Aurora, 20.4 inches in Boston, 18.3 inches in Perrysburg, 16.5 inches in Forestville, 16.4 inches in Silver Creek, 14 inches in Kendall, 13.9 inches in Attica, 12.8 inches in Cassadaga, 12.8 inches in Lakeside, 11.6 inches in Newfane, 10.3 inches in Cattaraugus, 10 inches in Orwell, 9 inches in Albion, 9 inches in Pulaski, 9 inches in Rochester, 8.7 inches in Pendleton, 8.3 inches in Brockport and 8 inches in Webster.

This was a more widespread event than the typical lake effect event with many more counties impacted and with warnings in effect compared to normal. ||Snowfall amounts for this event include: 41 inches in Hooker, 40.3 inches in Adams, 40 inches in Barnes Corners, 39.2 inches in Henderson Harbor, 27.4 inches in Watertown, 22 inches in Eden, 21 inches in East Aurora, 20.4 inches in Boston, 18.3 inches in Perrysburg, 16.5 inches in Forestville, 16.4 inches in Silver Creek, 14 inches in Kendall, 13.9 inches in Attica, 12.8 inches in Cassadaga, 12.8 inches in Lakeside, 11.6 inches in Newfane, 10.3 inches in Cattaraugus, 10 inches in Orwell, 9 inches in Albion, 9 inches in Pulaski, 9 inches in Rochester, 8.7 inches in Pendleton, 8.3 inches in Brockport and 8 inches in Webster.

An area of low pressure tracking from the Carolinas northeastward off the East Coast brought a widespread light to moderate snowfall to eastern New York on January 19-20, 2025. The greatest snowfall amounts occurred across areas mainly south of Interstate 90 and near the New York/New England border where 3 to 7 inches of snow were common. Farther north, lighter amounts occurred with values generally 4 inches or less. Slippery/icy travel occurred as a result of the snowfall.||Key impacts: travel delays.

An arctic airmass over the Lower Great Lakes developed a lake aggregate surface trough at the start of the event, which resulted in the development of a mesolow over the Niagara Frontier late on the night of the 19th. Areas east of Lake Erie received over 18 inches of snow, while areas east of Lake Ontario picked up over three feet of snow. This was a more widespread event than the typical lake effect event with many more counties impacted and with warnings in effect compared to normal. ||Snowfall amounts for this event include: 41 inches in Hooker, 40.3 inches in Adams, 40 inches in Barnes Corners, 39.2 inches in Henderson Harbor, 27.4 inches in Watertown, 22 inches in Eden, 21 inches in East Aurora, 20.4 inches in Boston, 18.3 inches in Perrysburg, 16.5 inches in Forestville, 16.4 inches in Silver Creek, 14 inches in Kendall, 13.9 inches in Attica, 12.8 inches in Cassadaga, 12.8 inches in Lakeside, 11.6 inches in Newfane, 10.3 inches in Cattaraugus, 10 inches in Orwell, 9 inches in Albion, 9 inches in Pulaski, 9 inches in Rochester, 8.7 inches in Pendleton, 8.3 inches in Brockport and 8 inches in Webster.

A multi-day lake effect snow event began on January 1, 2025 and continued into the evening hours on January 5, 2025. Low pressure tracked northward across the region on January 1 bringing a widespread rainfall, which gradually mixed with and changed over to snow during the afternoon and evening hours as colder air filtered back into the region. This low then tracked into Quebec and remained nearly stationary into January 3, 2025. Persistent west to west-northwesterly flow and a multi-lake connection during this time resulted in an oscillating band of heavy lake effect snow mostly impacting the Adirondacks and Mohawk Valley. ||An upper level disturbance tracked through the region during the evening hours on January 3, 2025 weakening and disrupting the lake effect response into the early morning hours on January 4 as flow became more northwesterly. West to west-northwesterly flow and a multi-lake connection resumed by the afternoon hours on January 4 as another period of heavy lake-effect snow took shape across the Adirondacks and Mohawk Valley which continued into January 5. A north to northwesterly wind shift and the arrival of surface high pressure and drier air ended the lake-effect event by the early evening hours on January 5.||Snowfall totals of 1 to locally 5 feet fell across the Adirondacks and Mohawk Valley during this 5-day event with the highest totals occurring across central portions of Herkimer County, just north of the New York State Thruway.||Snow emergencies were declared for some municipalities in southern Herkimer County including the villages of Ilion and Dolgeville.||Key impacts: travel delays.

A cold front pushed through, with a secondary cold frontal boundary lingering back to the west. Low pressure formed along the boundary and began to strengthen as it pulled away quickly to the east and northeast. The accumulating snow was generated mainly from frontogenetical forcing which occurred to the north and west of the developing surface low. Winter Storm criteria was reached in Orange and Putnam Counties, but fell just short of warning criteria across Northern Westchester. The remainder of SE New York either achieved advisory criteria or fell just short of advisory criteria.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained Spotters reported 8.5 inches in Washingtonville, 7.0 inches in Monroe, New Windsor, and Salisbury Mills.  Cocorahs reports included 8.3 inches at 4 SSE Chester, 7.1 inches in Warwick, 6.8 inches at Cornwall-on-Hudson, and 6.2 inches at Port Jervis.

With cold temperatures aloft (-14 degrees Celsius) and as a pre-frontal trough crossed the region, lake effect snow developed off of both lakes during the evening of January 23rd. Westerly flow between the passing trough and the cold front that crosses the region later that night resulted in most of the snow to focus on areas directly east of the lakes. Behind the passing cold front, northwest flow caused the lake effect snow to shift southeast of the lakes during the morning of January 24th; and from there the lake bands were less organized and movement of the lake bands limited most of the snowfall for any given location/area. Snowfall amounts for this short event weren���t overly impressive, but the greatest snowfall with this lake effect snow occurred east of Lake Ontario.||Snowfall amounts for this event include: 14.8 inches in Lacona, 10.5 inches in Richland, 9.5 inches in Fulton, 9.0 inches in Barnes Corners and 9.0 inches in Hooker.

A powerful area of low pressure tracked across northern Ontario and Quebec on January 27th and 28th. The strong cold front associated with the system tracked south across the western and north central New York area, causing moderate to heavy snowfall at times during the night of January 27th into the morning hours of January 28th. Lake enhancement and upsloping added to the snowfall amounts, especially northeast of Lake Ontario where up to a foot of snow accumulated. Because of the strength of the area of low pressure, dropping to as low as 966 hPa, a tight pressure gradient was present over the region.||Snowfall amounts for this event include: 12.0 inches in Lowville, 11.2 inches in Copenhagen, 10.2 inches in Castorland, 10.0 inches in Constableville and 9.0 inches in Calcium. ||Notable wind gusts for this event include: 66 mph at the Batavia New York State Mesonet, 63 mph at the Buffalo Airport, 61 mph at the Niagara Falls Airport, 60 mph at Dunkirk Airport and at the Fredonia C-Man station and 58 mph at the York New York State Mesonet.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Cocorahs observer reported 4.5 inches at 2.8 NNE Peekskill and 3.6 inches at Armonk early the following morning. Also during the evening a public report of 6.0 inches was received from 1 SE Croton-on-Hudson (the only one at 6 inches). A Cooperative observer reported 4.3 at Shrub Oak. Thus the far northern portions of the county did have a few reports close to 6 inches, but the zone easily averaged below warning criteria.

Winter Storm criteria was reached in Orange and Putnam Counties, but fell just short of warning criteria across Northern Westchester. The remainder of SE New York either achieved advisory criteria or fell just short of advisory criteria.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS Employees reported 3.2 inches in Stony Brook, 3.2 inches in Centereach, and 2.8 inches in Mt. Sinai. A Cooperative observer reported 3.6 inches in Centerport, with the public reporting 3.1 inches at 1 NNE Greenlawn, and 3.1 inches at 1 SSW Port Jefferson. Most of the zone, especially further north reached advisory criteria.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters reported 4.0 inches in Syosset, 2.5 inches in Levittown, with the public reporting 3.9 inches at East Williston, 3.9 inches at 1 W Plainview, and 3.8 inches at 1 ENE Glen Cove. A Cocorahs observer reported 2.7 inches in Manhasset Hills.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A multi-day lake effect snow event began on January 1, 2025 and continued into the evening hours on January 5, 2025. Low pressure tracked northward across the region on January 1 bringing a widespread rainfall, which gradually mixed with and changed over to snow during the afternoon and evening hours as colder air filtered back into the region. This low then tracked into Quebec and remained nearly stationary into January 3, 2025. Persistent west to west-northwesterly flow and a multi-lake connection during this time resulted in an oscillating band of heavy lake effect snow mostly impacting the Adirondacks and Mohawk Valley. ||An upper level disturbance tracked through the region during the evening hours on January 3, 2025 weakening and disrupting the lake effect response into the early morning hours on January 4 as flow became more northwesterly. West to west-northwesterly flow and a multi-lake connection resumed by the afternoon hours on January 4 as another period of heavy lake-effect snow took shape across the Adirondacks and Mohawk Valley which continued into January 5. A north to northwesterly wind shift and the arrival of surface high pressure and drier air ended the lake-effect event by the early evening hours on January 5.||Snowfall totals of 1 to locally 5 feet fell across the Adirondacks and Mohawk Valley during this 5-day event with the highest totals occurring across central portions of Herkimer County, just north of the New York State Thruway.||Snow emergencies were declared for some municipalities in southern Herkimer County including the villages of Ilion and Dolgeville.||Key impacts: travel delays.

A powerful area of low pressure tracked across northern Ontario and Quebec on January 27th and 28th. The strong cold front associated with the system tracked south across the western and north central New York area, causing moderate to heavy snowfall at times during the night of January 27th into the morning hours of January 28th. Lake enhancement and upsloping added to the snowfall amounts, especially northeast of Lake Ontario where up to a foot of snow accumulated. Because of the strength of the area of low pressure, dropping to as low as 966 hPa, a tight pressure gradient was present over the region.||Snowfall amounts for this event include: 12.0 inches in Lowville, 11.2 inches in Copenhagen, 10.2 inches in Castorland, 10.0 inches in Constableville and 9.0 inches in Calcium. ||Notable wind gusts for this event include: 66 mph at the Batavia New York State Mesonet, 63 mph at the Buffalo Airport, 61 mph at the Niagara Falls Airport, 60 mph at Dunkirk Airport and at the Fredonia C-Man station and 58 mph at the York New York State Mesonet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree downed in the road in Royalton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A multi-day lake effect snow event began on January 1, 2025 and continued into the evening hours on January 5, 2025. Low pressure tracked northward across the region on January 1 bringing a widespread rainfall, which gradually mixed with and changed over to snow during the afternoon and evening hours as colder air filtered back into the region. This low then tracked into Quebec and remained nearly stationary into January 3, 2025. Persistent west to west-northwesterly flow and a multi-lake connection during this time resulted in an oscillating band of heavy lake effect snow mostly impacting the Adirondacks and Mohawk Valley. ||An upper level disturbance tracked through the region during the evening hours on January 3, 2025 weakening and disrupting the lake effect response into the early morning hours on January 4 as flow became more northwesterly. West to west-northwesterly flow and a multi-lake connection resumed by the afternoon hours on January 4 as another period of heavy lake-effect snow took shape across the Adirondacks and Mohawk Valley which continued into January 5. A north to northwesterly wind shift and the arrival of surface high pressure and drier air ended the lake-effect event by the early evening hours on January 5.||Snowfall totals of 1 to locally 5 feet fell across the Adirondacks and Mohawk Valley during this 5-day event with the highest totals occurring across central portions of Herkimer County, just north of the New York State Thruway.||Snow emergencies were declared for some municipalities in southern Herkimer County including the villages of Ilion and Dolgeville.||Key impacts: travel delays.

A quick moving clipper system tracking directly across northern New York caused moderate to heavy snowfall across portions of the Buffalo forecast area. This was especially the case for areas east of Lake Ontario where closer proximity to the passing surface low, combined with lake enhancement and terrain influences caused over a foot of snow to accumulate in some areas. While snow was widespread across the entire Buffalo forecast area, the greatest amounts occurred over Lewis County. ||Snowfall amounts include: 16.5 inches in Constableville, 14.2 inches in Highmarket, 10.0 inches in Lowville and 9.0 inches in Chases Lake.

A long duration lake effect snow event brought significant snowfall, especially near and north of the NYS Thruway corridor. Snow started on Wednesday, the 1st, and then increased in intensity on Thursday, the 2nd, through midday Friday, the 3rd, as a multi-lake connection set up. A multi-lake connection returned at times through the weekend and moisture from the Atlantic was drawn into the region along the backside of a broad upper low over eastern Canada enhancing the lake effect. The main lake effect band fluctuated north and south at times and brought locally heavy bouts of snow several times across portions of central NY through the 5th before finally dissipating. The band resided the longest over northern Oneida County, where snow amounts as high as 65-75 inches were reported. Strong winds accompanied the lake effect snow at times leading to blowing and drifting snow and low visibility.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake effect snow accumulated between 24 and 75 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front, associated with an area of low pressure passing north of the Buffalo forecast area brought another round of colder air to support another lake effect event off of both lakes. The combination of synoptic moisture, large scale trough with passing shortwave troughs and the cold temperatures aloft resulted in another significant lake effect snow event. Most of the snow fell east to southeast of both lakes, with amounts off of both lakes nearing 30 inches by the time the event was over. ||Snowfall amounts for this event include: 29.4 inches in Highmarket, 29.0 inches in Glenwood, 28.8 inches in Colden, 27.0 inches in Constableville, 24.0 inches in Springville, 22.2 inches in Cattaraugus, 21.6 inches in Cassadaga, 21.0 inches in Worth, 20.0 inches in Dewittville, 18.1 inches in Warsaw, 16.2 inches in Palermo, 11.0 inches in Elma Center, 9.5 inches in Alexander.

Most of the snow fell east to southeast of both lakes, with amounts off of both lakes nearing 30 inches by the time the event was over. ||Snowfall amounts for this event include: 29.4 inches in Highmarket, 29.0 inches in Glenwood, 28.8 inches in Colden, 27.0 inches in Constableville, 24.0 inches in Springville, 22.2 inches in Cattaraugus, 21.6 inches in Cassadaga, 21.0 inches in Worth, 20.0 inches in Dewittville, 18.1 inches in Warsaw, 16.2 inches in Palermo, 11.0 inches in Elma Center, 9.5 inches in Alexander.

A west-east band of lake effect snow developed in the wake of a passing cold front on January 13, 2025 and continued into January 14 before dropping southward and weakening. The combination of radar precipitation estimates and snow depth increases at the New York State Mesonet at Old Forge verify that 7 or more inches of snow fell within areas along and north of Route 28 within northern Herkimer County.||Key impacts: travel delays.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The combination of radar precipitation estimates and snow depth increases at New York State Mesonet sites verify that 7 or more inches of snow fell within areas along and north of Route 28.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure tracking from the Carolinas northeastward off the East Coast brought a widespread light to moderate snowfall to eastern New York on January 19-20, 2025. The greatest snowfall amounts occurred across areas mainly south of Interstate 90 and near the New York/New England border where 3 to 7 inches of snow were common. Farther north, lighter amounts occurred with values generally 4 inches or less. Slippery/icy travel occurred as a result of the snowfall.||Key impacts: travel delays.

An arctic airmass over the Lower Great Lakes developed a lake aggregate surface trough at the start of the event, which resulted in the development of a mesolow over the Niagara Frontier late on the night of the 19th. Heavy snow with the mesolow stretched as far east as the Rochester Metro area. A deep mid and upper level trough and re-enforcing cold air continued the favorable conditions for lake effect snow to continue into the middle of the week. 850 hPa temperatures dropping to near -25 degrees Celsius resulted in plenty of instability over the lakes and combined with the passing troughs and west to west-southwest flow, areas east to east-northeast of the lakes received heavy snowfall during this event. Areas east of Lake Erie received over 18 inches of snow, while areas east of Lake Ontario picked up over three feet of snow. This was a more widespread event than the typical lake effect event with many more counties impacted and with warnings in effect compared to normal. ||Snowfall amounts for this event include: 41 inches in Hooker, 40.3 inches in Adams, 40 inches in Barnes Corners, 39.2 inches in Henderson Harbor, 27.4 inches in Watertown, 22 inches in Eden, 21 inches in East Aurora, 20.4 inches in Boston, 18.3 inches in Perrysburg, 16.5 inches in Forestville, 16.4 inches in Silver Creek, 14 inches in Kendall, 13.9 inches in Attica, 12.8 inches in Cassadaga, 12.8 inches in Lakeside, 11.6 inches in Newfane, 10.3 inches in Cattaraugus, 10 inches in Orwell, 9 inches in Albion, 9 inches in Pulaski, 9 inches in Rochester, 8.7 inches in Pendleton, 8.3 inches in Brockport and 8 inches in Webster.

A weak wave of low pressure developed and traveled along a stationary front draped across northern NY and VT during the evening of March 28th and continuing into the 29th.  Snowfall amounts ranged  from several inches across southern portions of northern NY to a half foot or more in the northern sections along the Canadian border. Meanwhile across the southern tier there was mixed winter precipitation, including freezing rain and icing that accumulated upwards of an half inch in some locations. Power outages across northern NY were around 5000.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few inches of snow combined with ice accumulations up to an half inch or slightly higher of flat ice.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong, westerly winds developed across eastern New York behind the passage of a cold front as amplifying surface high pressure approached from the Ohio Valley and a low pressure system strengthened east of the New England Coast. Peak wind gusts across the region ranged from around 30 to 50 miles per hour with a peak gust of 52 miles per hour in the town of Mechanicville in Saratoga County.

A low pressure system brought a period of snow changing to a wintry mix from south to north across eastern New York during the morning hours on February 6, 2025. Snowfall amounts generally ranged from a coating to 2 inches with a light glaze of ice resulting in slippery travel conditions, especially during the morning commute.||Key impacts: travel delays.

A dual area of low pressure moved across northern NY and New England on February 13th delivering primarily snow but a period of mixed wintry precipitation in the form of sleet and freezing rain as well.||Combined snow and sleet accumulations were largely 3 to 5 inches across the region with typical travel related impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A widespread snow mixed with sleet and freezing rain at times with snowfall accumulations of 2 to 5 inches across the area.

A significant and elongated lake effect snow band developed off Lake Ontario in the late afternoon to early evening of February 13th in the wake of a low pressure system and its attendant cold front. The primary band extended south and eastward, cutting through southern Herkimer, Montgomery, Schoharie, Albany, Greene, and Rensselaer Counties. Additional bandelettes also developed off of Lake Ontario through the afternoon of February 14th, leading to additional accumulations. Snowfall accumulations were highly variable across the impacted area with the highest amount of 18 inches reported in far southern Herkimer County to the lowest report of a mere 0.1 inches in Montgomery County. Additionally, upslope enhancement in the higher terrain regions of the Helderbergs and Eastern Catskills led to some locally higher totals of 6 to 8 inches in western Albany and Schoharie Counties. ||Key Impacts: travel delays, slippery travel conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The greatest snowfall total in southern Herkimer County reported by a local trained spotter was 18 inches near the town of Frankfort. Another warning-level total was 11.5 inches observed by another trained spotter in the Village of Ilion.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals across the area were generally 3 to 5 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals across Essex county ranged from 4 to 6 inches in northern sections with 6 to 8 inches in southern sections.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure developed in the lower Mississippi River Valley on the evening of February 15th (Saturday) and then moved northeast across the Ohio River Valley midday on February 16th (Sunday) and into the Gulf of Maine during the evening hours.||A large pressure gradient led to strong southeast winds on the front end of the storm with cold and equally strong northwest winds after storm passage into Newfoundland.||Snow overspread the region Saturday afternoon, snowing heavy at times during Sunday before ending Sunday evening. Some sleet mixed in at times Sunday across southern areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions locally developed in the northern St. Lawrence river valley during a larger winter storm between 10 am and 2 pm. Northeast winds of 20 to 30 mph with frequent gusts over 40 mph, including gusts around 50 mph combined with falling and blowing snow with visibilities 1/4 of a mile or lower during this period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure developed in the lower Mississippi River Valley on the evening of February 15th (Saturday) and then moved northeast across the Ohio River Valley midday on February 16th (Sunday) and into the Gulf of Maine during the evening hours.||A large pressure gradient led to strong southeast winds on the front end of the storm with cold and equally strong northwest winds after storm passage into Newfoundland.||Snow overspread the region Saturday afternoon, snowing heavy at times during Sunday before ending Sunday evening. Some sleet mixed in at times Sunday across southern areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous wind gusts in excess of 40 mph were recorded and combined with freshly fallen snow for considerable blowing and drifting snow that caused some driving hazards of poor visibility and snow covered roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracking south of the region brought a period of moderate to heavy snowfall across eastern New York from the evening hours on February 8 through the morning hours on February 9, 2025 before ending. The heaviest snowfall amounts occurred across areas north of Interstate 90 where 6 to 12 inches were common. Lighter accumulations occurred along and south of Interstate 90 with amounts between 2 and 6 inches, with some patchy light freezing drizzle also occurring during the overnight hours.||Key impacts: travel delays.

A significant and elongated lake effect snow band developed off Lake Ontario in the late afternoon to early evening of February 13th in the wake of a low pressure system and its attendant cold front. The primary band extended south and eastward, cutting through southern Herkimer, Montgomery, Schoharie, Albany, Greene, and Rensselaer Counties. Additional bandelettes also developed off of Lake Ontario through the afternoon of February 14th, leading to additional accumulations. Snowfall accumulations were highly variable across the impacted area with the highest amount of 18 inches reported in far southern Herkimer County to the lowest report of a mere 0.1 inches in Montgomery County. Additionally, upslope enhancement in the higher terrain regions of the Helderbergs and Eastern Catskills led to some locally higher totals of 6 to 8 inches in western Albany and Schoharie Counties. ||Key Impacts: travel delays, slippery travel conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake effect snow bands reached western Albany County and let to snowfall amounts of 2 to 6 inches with the highest totals in the higher terrain of the Helderbergs courtesy of upslope enhancement.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved across Quebec trailing an associated cold front in which a weak area of low pressure developed and traveled across New England on the night of February 3rd.||Mixed rain and snow developed during the afternoon of February 3rd and changed to snow during the evening hours before ending during the early morning hours of February 4th. ||The initial rain and snow mixed, followed by falling temperatures and snow created an icy layer that resulted in treacherous roads during the night and early morning hours. Snowfall totals were generally 3 to 5 inches across northern NY with 1 to 2 inches across the Champlain Valley of NY.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals across the area were generally 2 to 4 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure lifted northeast out of the Tennessee Valley and tracked just west of the area with the warm front getting up against south and southeastern portions of the region. A light accumulating snow changed to a prolonged period of freezing rain across northern interior sections.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Montgomery ASOS reported 0.52 inches of ice and a trained spotter reported 0.31 inches of ice in Monroe. With radar estimates while temperatures were below freezing along with impacts, warning criteria was estimated to be reached across the northern half of the zone. 1 to 2 inches of snow fell across the central and northern portions of the zone before the change to freezing rain. Large branches were reported down in Chester with some scattered outages across north central Orange County.

A progressive low pressure system emerged out of the Tennessee Valley and quickly moved across the region bringing an accumulating wet snowfall. Most places received an advisory level snowfall with mainly 3 to 4 inches of snow, with some locations getting slightly higher and lower amounts. This was a front end quick thump driven by warm advection snowfall which fell primarily during the evening hours of the 8th. The snow quickly tapered to some light freezing rain and drizzle in spots after the initial thump. Some locations further east changed back to a lighter snow before ending in the early morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mainly 3 to 4 inches fell across the zone with the majority of the accumulating snow falling during the late evening hours. Generally 3 to 4 inches were reported across the zone. Trained spotters reported 4.1 inches in Washingtonville, 3.8 inches at 1 WNW Monroe, and 3.0 inches at 2 SSW Harriman.  Cocorahs observers reported 3.9 inches at Cornwall on the Hudson, 3.8 inches at 4 SSE Chester, and 3.1 inches at Warwick.

A storm system tracking out of the Tennessee Valley northeast to Central and Eastern New York brought a variety of winter weather to the Buffalo forecast area. While areas south and southeast of Lake Ontario changed to rain and received less winter weather overall, Jefferson and Lewis counties took the brunt of the snow and ice associated with the system. The track and proximity of the storm system allowed enough warm air to push north into the southern portion of the Buffalo forecast area to change the snow to rain. Freezing rain and snow was the primary precipitation type for Lewis and Jefferson county, where Winter Storm Warnings were in effect for this event. ||Snowfall amounts for this event include: 14.8 inches in La Fargeville, 11.7 inches in Copenhagen, and 8.0 inches in Lowville.

Low pressure developed in the lower Mississippi River Valley on the evening of February 15th (Saturday) and then moved northeast across the Ohio River Valley midday on February 16th (Sunday) and into the Gulf of Maine during the evening hours.||A large pressure gradient led to strong southeast winds on the front end of the storm with cold and equally strong northwest winds after storm passage into Newfoundland.||Snow overspread the region Saturday afternoon, snowing heavy at times during Sunday before ending Sunday evening. Some sleet mixed in at times Sunday across southern areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals across the county were 8 to 15 inches with brisk winds developing in the evening hours to begin blowing and drifting snow that caused some driving hazards of poor visibility and snow covered roads through February 17th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals across the county were 10 to 18 inches with brisk winds developing in the evening hours to begin blowing and drifting snow that caused some driving hazards of poor visibility and snow covered roads through February 17th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A progressive low pressure system emerged out of the Tennessee Valley and quickly moved across the region bringing an accumulating wet snowfall. Most places received an advisory level snowfall with mainly 3 to 4 inches of snow, with some locations getting slightly higher and lower amounts. This was a front end quick thump driven by warm advection snowfall which fell primarily during the evening hours of the 8th. The snow quickly tapered to some light freezing rain and drizzle in spots after the initial thump. Some locations further east changed back to a lighter snow before ending in the early morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An accumulating snowfall of mainly 3 to 4 inches occurred across the county / zone. Cocorahs observers reported 3.9 inches in Stony Point and 3.2 inches at 1.7 SSW Spring  Valley. A trained spotter reported 3.5 inches in New City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak area of low pressure and associated front moved across northern NY during the afternoon and evening hours of May 17th. This system moved across a relatively unstable airmass across the region that resulted in numerous showers and thunderstorms.||Some of the thunderstorms were severe with damaging winds knocking down several trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree down on high tension lines and blocking the road near the intersection of Eddy/North star and Farrand roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of severe thunderstorms that initially developed over Wisconsin the previous evening tracked all the way to the western New York area through the night. The line developed ahead of an occluded front within a unstable and sheared environment. As the storms tracked into western New York, conditions became less favorable to maintain the strength of the storms. However, upon initially entering the western New York area, the thunderstorms were still strong to severe before weakening to just below severe thunderstorm criteria further east.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree downed on Prospect Road in Westfield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the early morning hours of Saturday May 17th, scattered thunderstorms along a warm front tracking east across Lake Ontario continued into the north central New York area. As the storms tracked inland from the lake, they became better organized and became severe. ||Later in the morning and into the early afternoon, a pre-frontal trough, ahead of the cold front associated with the surface low and earlier warm front tracked across the area. As the trough tracked into far western New York, thunderstorms developed along it, and intensified to severe criteria.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires downed on Shaw Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe discrete thunderstorms were initiated along a slow moving frontal boundary in an area with about 1000 J/kg of SBCAPE. They were able to become somewhat organized given the 40 kt of effective bulk shear that was available due to a belt of southwesterly flow aloft. The storms produced damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees and powerlines down near Unionville NY.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to locally damaging winds developed across eastern New York in the wake of a cold front beginning in the afternoon hours on March 6, 2025 and continued into March 7, 2025. Widespread wind gusts between 40 and 60 mph were observed with some locally higher gusts including a 69 mph wind gust at the Schenectady County Airport. These winds resulted in some downed trees and wires across the area. Strong winds continued on March 8 but were not as strong with some peak gusts between 40 and 50 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 44 mph wind gust was recorded at the New York State Mesonet site near Stephentown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On March 16, a strong area of low pressure tracked across the Central Great Lakes into southeast Ontario and southwest Quebec. The warm front associated with the system tracked north across the western New York area during the morning hours and an associated low level jet increased winds out of the south, especially for downslope areas near Lake Erie. This resulted in non thunderstorm winds that caused damage across much of Chautauqua County prior to the arrival of severe thunderstorms during the early afternoon. Wind Advisories or Warnings were in effect for all of the western and north central New York counties due to the potential for gusty winds. Convection developed across Ontario and Pennsylvania during the late morning hours along a pre-frontal trough associated with the system. As the pre-frontal trough and early convection tracked into and then across the western New York area, conditions were favorable for strong to severe thunderstorms to develop. Severe thunderstorms caused damage across much of the Buffalo forecast area, mainly inland from Lake Erie and extending to the North Country. ||In addition to the damage noted above across Chautauqua County, a measured wind gust of 66 MPH occurred at the Dunkirk Airport during the morning of March 16.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree down on wires in North Chili.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area low pressure moved over the Great Lakes region and a warm front lifting north early in the day put the area into the warm sector of the system, ahead of an approaching cold front. While instability was limited, there was strong wind shear in place, and a line of gusty showers and thunderstorms along and ahead of the cold front produced locally damaging wind gusts. A funnel cloud embedded within the line was briefly observed south of the Syracuse area. There was also a tight pressure gradient in place which resulted in strong, locally damaging non-thunderstorm synoptic winds prior to the cold frontal passage as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down numerous trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Local officials reported an ice jam along Great Gully Brook near where the brook enters into Cayuga Lake. Flooding extended along Great Gully Cove Road and Fire Lane number 8 from the walking bridge to the lake shore. Excavators were brought into the scene to assist with disrupting the jam and mitigating flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding from a large ice jam affected two properties along Great Gully Cove Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of precipitation moved across eastern New York on March 29-30, 2025. A wedge of cold air near the surface led to rounds of freezing rain primarily across the Adirondacks and Upper Hudson Valley. Flat ice accumulations in excess of 0.50 inches occurred in parts of the area resulting in high accretion on trees and power lines. Some trees and wires were downed resulting in some power outages.||Key impacts: travel delays, power outages.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds produced a 58 mph gust at KELM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On March 16, a strong area of low pressure tracked across the Central Great Lakes into southeast Ontario and southwest Quebec. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light snow fell during the morning hours on the 7th. The fresh snow combined with strong winds led to blowing and drifting snow and whiteout conditions at times into the afternoon. Wind gusts as high as 50 mph were recorded at KRME early in the afternoon. Shortly before 3 PM, two school buses collided head-on in the town of Floyd on Rickmeyer Road killing one of the drivers. Three other individuals were transported to the hospital for non-life threatening injuries. The peak wind gust recorded around this time was 48 mph and local law enforcement reported that whiteout conditions was a factor in the crash.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two school buses collided head-on in whiteout conditions, killing one of the drivers. Three other individuals were treated for non-life threatening injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Wind and snow-covered roads were factors in a two vehicle crash that occurred around 5:30pm on the 8th on Route 8 in Paris, NY. A driver lost control of their vehicle on snow-covered roads, crossing the center line and collided with an oncoming SUV. A fresh round of light snow had fallen during the early morning hours and winds were gusting on average 25 to 35 mph throughout the day leading to blowing and drifting snow. One passenger died from their injuries 16 days after the crash, while five others were treated for injuries at a local hospital.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A driver lost control of their vehicle on snow-covered roads, crossing the center line and collided with an oncoming SUV. Six people were initially injured, but one passed away from their injuries on the 24th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area low pressure moved over the Great Lakes region and a warm front lifting north early in the day put the area into the warm sector of the system, ahead of an approaching cold front. While instability was limited, there was strong wind shear in place, and a line of gusty showers and thunderstorms along and ahead of the cold front produced locally damaging wind gusts. There was also a tight pressure gradient in place which resulted in strong, locally damaging non-thunderstorm synoptic winds prior to the cold frontal passage as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down 10 to 12 trees near Hillendale Golf Course.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Wintry weather developed across portions of the Buffalo forecast area as a stationary front draped from west to east provided forcing and a sharp thermal gradient across the region. While mixed precipitation prompted Winter Weather Advisories for counties along the south shore of Lake Ontario and for the North Country, an Ice Storm Warning was necessary for Jefferson County. Northeast drainage flow from the St. Lawrence Valley, provided the cooler low level temperatures to keep surface temperatures below freezing for much longer in areas of far northern Jefferson County, especially north of Route 11 & Route 12. With the steady precipitation and the above freezing temperatures aloft, rain fell into the sub-freezing surface layer, freezing on contact with the ground, trees and other elevated surfaces. Freezing rain/ice accumulations of at least a half inch were reported for a few spots in Northern Jefferson County and numerous trees and wires were downed in these areas well, resulting in at least 2,500 customers without power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain accumulation of 0.50 reported by two different sources one in Clayton and the other near Redwood. Additionally, numerous trees and wires were downed in the northern portion of Jefferson county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold air filtered into eastern New York during the early morning hours on March 2, 2025 behind a strong cold front. Wind chill (or feels-like temperatures) fell to between 20 and 25 below zero across portions of the southern Adirondacks.

A strong area low pressure moved over the Great Lakes region and a warm front lifting north early in the day put the area into the warm sector of the system, ahead of an approaching cold front. While instability was limited, there was strong wind shear in place, and a line of gusty showers and thunderstorms along and ahead of the cold front produced locally damaging wind gusts. A funnel cloud embedded within the line was briefly observed south of the Syracuse area. There was also a tight pressure gradient in place which resulted in strong, locally damaging non-thunderstorm synoptic winds prior to the cold frontal passage as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down trees. A brush fire also started.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A MCS (Mesoscale Convective System) traveled along the heat ridge across the eastern half of the CONUS across southern Canada and then moved southeast across the Lake Ontario region, including clipping southwest St. Lawrence county where thunderstorm winds in excess of 60 mph were observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In addition to the observed wind gusts, there were several branches broken from trees and yard debris thrown around.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A triple point between an occluded, cold and warm fronts extending south from a surface low across James Bay Canada moved across northern NY during the afternoon hours of June 10th. The airmass was marginally unstable with a few showers and an isolated thunderstorm or two. One thunderstorm briefly intensified and may have interacted with a sea-breeze front off of Lake Champlain that produced a very brief EF0 tornado in Beekmantown with several trees downed, some yard objects lifted and moved and a partial unstable roof torn off a house.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports of multiple downed trees were  investigated and confirmed an EF-0 briefly  touched down. Trees were uprooted and snapped. Part of a roof was peeled and wrapped around a powerline. Small objects,  like a trampoline, were flung.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front was the causing factor of showers and early afternoon thunderstorms across eastern New York on Saturday, May 3, 2025. While showers were more widespread throughout the region, thunderstorm activity became more concentrated within the Mid-Hudson Valley and Taconics where instability within the warm sector of the parent low pressure system rose more significantly. A couple of individual storms in the Mid-Hudson Valley achieved severe strength with 1 inch hail being reported. Another storm, a linear convective system, in particular also became severe in strength, inflicting wind damage to trees and powerlines across the lower Mid-Hudson Valley and Taconics. Several reports were conveyed to NWS Albany of downed trees and wires blocking roadways and causing temporary closures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 911 call center operator reported a tree down across Amenia Union Road near Webatuck Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system interacting with a stationary frontal system draped across southeast New York triggered copious amounts of showers and thunderstorms. An enhanced area of prolonged heavy rainfall over Delaware county produced a period of excessive runoff and some flash flooding of local roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding from East Brook was affecting county road CR-22.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system remained nearly stationary over the Ohio Valley. Weak forcing associated with this feature coupled with deep southeasterly flow off the Atlantic Ocean to provide the area with persistent showers and scattered thunderstorms. Locally heavy rainfall concentrated over portions of Steuben county causing some streams and creeks to exceed their banks and impact area roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding closed State Route 36 and 248 with water flowing over portions of these roads near Canisteo.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled frontal boundary over the Finger Lakes region helped to trigger scattered thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening. Instability was modest yet supportive of effectiving mixing down of strong winds. There was also plenty of wind shear aloft. This allowed for a few storms to become strong to severe resulting in locally damaging winds and hail. It was determined that a microburst also occurred in Schuyler County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down multiple trees and wires throughout Cortland.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A microburst occurred near Monterey which produced estimated peak wind speeds of 85 mph. Multiple properties were damaged, including the collapse of a wooden garage, major loss of a metal and wooden barn, major loss of metal roofing on another barn, and a hole from a 2-inch diameter branch that punctured through two doors of a house. Evidence of hail damage was seen on a house with multiple small holes on the|siding, and copious amounts of hail at least one inch in diameter. After the focused microburst ended, this storm progressed eastward with additional pockets of|estimated wind speeds of 65 to 75 mph, leading to numerous uprooted and snapped trees along a path from southern Montour Falls to Odessa and Alpine. Some of these trees fell onto properties, and a downed electrical wire caused an injury as an individual came into contact with the live wire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving upper level low pressure system interacted with a surface frontal boundary to induce torrential rain-producing thunderstorms. These storms moved into Central New York during the afternoon and repeatedly struck the same areas leading to localized flash flooding of roads, ditches and urban areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was flowing over a roadway in Mottville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled frontal boundary over the Finger Lakes region helped to trigger scattered thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening. Instability was modest yet supportive of effectiving mixing down of strong winds. There was also plenty of wind shear aloft. This allowed for a few storms to become strong to severe resulting in locally damaging winds and hail. It was determined that a microburst also occurred in Schuyler County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds caused a silo to collapse on the corner of Sebring Road, County Road 13, and Mezourek Road.
