EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the early morning hours of Saturday May 17th, scattered thunderstorms along a warm front tracking east across Lake Ontario continued into the north central New York area. As the storms tracked inland from the lake, they became better organized and became severe. ||Later in the morning and into the early afternoon, a pre-frontal trough, ahead of the cold front associated with the surface low and earlier warm front tracked across the area. As the trough tracked into far western New York, thunderstorms developed along it, and intensified to severe criteria.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree downed on wires in Hastings.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure and a frontal boundary lifted north across the area during the afternoon and evening drawing up plenty of moisture from the south and increasing instability. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure and a frontal boundary lifted north across the area during the afternoon and evening drawing up plenty of moisture from the south and increasing instability. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm moved through parts of the Town of Hancock. This storm produced a weak microburst, which resulted in uprooted trees and some snapped smaller trees along its path. Some of the uprooted trees may have also been aided by saturated soil conditions. The first visible tree damage was located in a wooded area, before the thunderstorm continued to move northward and crossed Bodoit Road, resulting in more uprooted trees along the road and at some residents' yards along the road. The storm then crossed the East Branch Delaware River and uprooted several more trees on |a small island in the middle of the river. More tree damage and damage to a power pole bracket was observed along Old Route 17 and along NY State Highway 17, before the storm tracked over hills north of NY State Highway 17. The last observed tree damage was located along Tar Hollow Road. Additional tree damage was observed via drone images.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hamilton Street has flooded according to law enforcement.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a couple potent, upper-level disturbances and a pre-frontal surface trough inflicted scattered severe thunderstorms across eastern New York on Saturday, May 17. Numerous reports of tree and power line damage were relayed to NWS Albany, mainly within and north of the Capital District, which led to the temporary closures of several roadways. However, an isolated storm in the Taconics of Columbia County developed in the evening, also producing some tree damage of its own. In addition to wind damage, large hail was also commonly reported across the Greater Capital Region. Hail stones measuring one inch in diameter was especially common in Saratoga and Schenectady Counties, with a report of a one and three quarters inches in diameter hail stone in Schenectady County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A report of one inch hail near Corinth was received from a member of the public on social media.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down a tree on wires.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough of low pressure and associated cold front moved through the area during the afternoon and evening into an environment with sufficient instability and shear, along with supportive conditions for mixing down of strong winds. A strong outflow boundary from one of the storms produced several reports of damage as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds uprooted a tree which fell down onto a house and wires were involved.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow-moving thunderstorms resulted in flooding and flash flooding across portions of southern Herkimer County during the afternoon and early evening hours on May 6, 2025. Flooding was mostly concentrated within the villages of Ilion, Mohawk and Herkimer, where 2 to 3 inches of rain fell in a 1 to 2 hour period. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intensifying coastal low pressure system brought prolonged moderate to locally heavy rainfall beginning early Saturday, May 31 through that same afternoon before lightening and gradually tapering off in the evening and into the overnight. Rainfall totals across eastern New York ranged from around one inch to three and a half inches with a maximum total of 3.95 inches in Ulster County. A couple of reports of poor drainage flooding were relayed to NWS Albany with the most significant impact being the closure of NY295 going both directions between Rod and Gun Club Road and Hartigan Road in Chatham in Columbia County. Additionally, the combination of strong synoptic winds and very moist soils led to a handful of reports of downed trees and wires, specifically in Dutchess County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter in the Town of Hillsdale reported 3.2 inches of rain as of 0935 AM EST.  This spotter also reported that culverts are flooded and that debris and sediment is causing minor flooding on Pheasant Lane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intensifying coastal low pressure system brought prolonged moderate to locally heavy rainfall beginning early Saturday, May 31 through that same afternoon before lightening and gradually tapering off in the evening and into the overnight. Rainfall totals across eastern New York ranged from around one inch to three and a half inches with a maximum total of 3.95 inches in Ulster County. A couple of reports of poor drainage flooding were relayed to NWS Albany with the most significant impact being the closure of NY295 going both directions between Rod and Gun Club Road and Hartigan Road in Chatham in Columbia County. Additionally, the combination of strong synoptic winds and very moist soils led to a handful of reports of downed trees and wires, specifically in Dutchess County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A New York Alert from the Department of Transportation reported the flooding and closure  of NY 295 going both directions between Rod and Gun Club Road and Hartigan Road in  Chatham in Columbia County. Both lanes of this road were closed as of 1121 AM EST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a couple potent, upper-level disturbances and a pre-frontal surface trough inflicted scattered severe thunderstorms across eastern New York on Saturday, May 17. Numerous reports of tree and power line damage were relayed to NWS Albany, mainly within and north of the Capital District, which led to the temporary closures of several roadways. However, an isolated storm in the Taconics of Columbia County developed in the evening, also producing some tree damage of its own. In addition to wind damage, large hail was also commonly reported across the Greater Capital Region. Hail stones measuring one inch in diameter was especially common in Saratoga and Schenectady Counties, with a report of a one and three quarters inches in diameter hail stone in Schenectady County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 911 call center operator reported a tree and wires down on Eastside Drive in Ballston Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow-moving thunderstorms resulted in flooding and flash flooding across portions of southern Herkimer County during the afternoon and early evening hours on May 6, 2025. Flooding was mostly concentrated within the villages of Ilion, Mohawk and Herkimer, where 2 to 3 inches of rain fell in a 1 to 2 hour period. While most of the flooding occurred with an initial batch of thunderstorms, an additional round of heavy rain in the early evening hours led to some renewed flooding from Frankfort to Herkimer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms developed across western New York and Lake Erie during the late afternoon on Thursday May 1st as a warm front slowly tracked northeast across the area. The thunderstorms became strong enough to cause a few trees to fall in the Jamestown area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of multiple trees downed in Jamestown area, including a tree in Lakewood that was downed on a house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving area of low pressure brought periods of steady rain to western New York. Embedded thunderstorms within the steadier rain caused heavier downpours at times during the evening on May 5th. Heavier rain, combined with the periods of steady rain resulted in Flash Flooding in Allegany and Livingston counties. The Town of Canaseraga experienced the brunt of the flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several homes evacuated on River Street due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Tuesday April 29, a cold front crossed the region causing showers and thunderstorms to develop. Conditions and timing were favorable ahead of the front as it approached the Buffalo forecast area with CAPE values of 1,000+ J/kg and bulk shear values of 40+ kts. Numerous reports of wind damage and severe hail were received for areas south and southeast of Lake Ontario.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball sized hail reported in Warsaw.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal trough approached at the surface out ahead of a cold front. Aloft, a shortwave trough led to falling heights into the afternoon. These features combined with MLCAPE values between 1000 and 2000 J/kg and moderate shear allowed for thunderstorms to develop and become organized. These storms produced damaging wind gusts and frequent cloud to ground lightning in southeast NY.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree and wires down across 60th Place and Flushing Avenue in Maspeth.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms developed along a cold front and within the warm sector of an approaching system. Favorable shear, along with forcing from the cold front helped to develop severe storms that also resulted in a confirmed tornado in the western Southern Tier. Additionally, a very moist atmosphere was present within the systems warm sector, helping to contribute to heavy rainfall with showers and thunderstorms. Some of the heavy rain that developed across the western Southern Tier, combined with antecedent conditions caused flash flooding in Allegany County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The National Weather Service in Buffalo investigated storm-related damage from a rotating thunderstorm that affected the area around Great Valley in Cattaraugus County on June 9. During the event, radar signatures indicated debris from the storm had|lofted into the atmosphere. This allowed for radar confirmation of a tornado. The National Weather Service storm survey team investigated the damage in the area today and confirmed damage consistent with an EF-1 tornado with estimated peak winds of|110 mph. ||Damage from the tornado generally followed Sugartown Road and Forks Creek. The initial touchdown and first damage occurred in the area between U.S. Highway 219 and Farm Market Road and continued northeast for approximately 2.4 miles. Initial damage from the storm included some hardwood trees north of Highway 98 and some facade damage to homes immediately south of Highway 98. Additional damage near initial touchdown included the loss of the roof of a garage. As the tornado continued northeast, damage quickly became more significant. Several homes, garages, and structures near the intersection of Sugartown Road with Farm Market Road, Haines Hollow Road, and Bonnie Way either had the full loss of the structure, loss of significant|roofing material, or the loss of some of the walls in the upper portion of the structure. The more modern construction fared substantially better than the majority of the structures in this area. Additionally, trees on the entire hillside north of the intersection were entirely sheared off mid-trunk for hundreds up yards uphill from the structures.||The tornadic damage continued northeast with a clearly-defined path of damage across McGuan Road of sheared tree trunks and sporadic structural damage to a few structures along the way. Tree damage became less severe after this point with intermittent tree damage continuing along the path until the tornado dissipated near Forks Creek in the Town of Humphrey.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front tracking east across the western and north central New York area on August 24th, caused strong to severe thunderstorms to develop. Severe thunderstorms resulted in wind damage and large hail for portions of the area east of the Genesee Valley.  Thunderstorms initially developed over the eastern end of Lake Erie during the morning hours and strengthened and expanded in coverage through the morning and into the afternoon as the cold front and thunderstorms pushed east. Increasing instability and shear ahead of the front, along with the forcing from the front caused the thunderstorms to reach severe limits.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several Trees downed in Port Byron.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent upper-level disturbance, together with a northward-tracking surface low from the Mid-Atlantic region brought light to moderate snowfall to portions of eastern New York from late April 11 through early afternoon April 12. The heaviest snowfall was concentrated south of Interstate 90 and confined to the higher terrain regions of the Eastern Catskills, Helderbergs, and Taconics. Accumulations of one half inch to 6 inches were common within and south of the Capital Region, with much of this falling during the overnight period of April 11. As this occurred over a weekend, little impact to travel occurred, though slippery road conditions did occur. ||Key impacts: slippery road conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Zone average snowfall of 2.5 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal trough tracking across the Buffalo forecast area during the late morning and early afternoon of June 28th caused thunderstorms to develop in some areas. Thunderstorms that developed over Lake Ontario during the late morning hours became better organized and became strong to severe over the far eastern end of Lake Ontario, further strengthening over north central New York during the early afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree down on County Road 22 near Canfield Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through the area during the afternoon and evening on the 19th into a high shear environment. Unseasonable warmth with highs in the 70s combined with modest amounts of instability led to the development of a line of showers and thunderstorms ahead of the boundary. A few storms embedded in the line became severe producing isolated wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked a tree down across the roadway at Route 97 and Viaduct Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms developed along a cold front and within the warm sector of an approaching system. Favorable shear, along with forcing from the cold front helped to develop severe storms that also resulted in a confirmed tornado in the western Southern Tier. Additionally, a very moist atmosphere was present within the systems warm sector, helping to contribute to heavy rainfall with showers and thunderstorms. Some of the heavy rain that developed across the western Southern Tier, combined with antecedent conditions caused flash flooding in Allegany County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires down in Town of Pulaski.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed along a Lake Erie lake breeze boundary during the late morning of June 18th. With the very high moisture laden airmass over the area, heavy rain occurred within some of the showers and thunderstorms that developed. Some of the thunderstorms, especially early, trained over the same area long enough to cause flash flooding issues. Additional showers and thunderstorms developed during the afternoon ahead of a slow moving cold front over Lake Ontario, causing additional heavy rainfall for portions of the area. Most of the flooding occurred in portions of the western Southern Tier and in the northern Finger Lakes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several reports of flooded roads in Canandaigua.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the afternoon of June 27th, strong to severe thunderstorms developed along a warm front slowly tracking northeast across the western New York area. Some of the thunderstorms that developed also produced heavy rainfall, leading to flash flooding in some areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and evening of the 29th along and ahead of a strong cold front that swept through the area. An increase in moisture, shear and instability during the afternoon and temperatures rising into the upper 70s to lower 80s led to strong to severe thunderstorms developing with damaging winds being the main impact. Thunderstorms started off cellular in nature before ending as a more organized line of showers and storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms winds resulted in a swath of damage 1 to 3 miles north of Himrod. A roof was blown off in one piece from a farmhouse, the metal chute on the side of a silo had minor damage, numerous trees were knocked down and a set of power poles were broken.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed along a Lake Erie lake breeze boundary during the late morning of June 18th. With the very high moisture laden airmass over the area, heavy rain occurred within some of the showers and thunderstorms that developed. Some of the thunderstorms, especially early, trained over the same area long enough to cause flash flooding issues. Additional showers and thunderstorms developed during the afternoon ahead of a slow moving cold front over Lake Ontario, causing additional heavy rainfall for portions of the area. Most of the flooding occurred in portions of the western Southern Tier and in the northern Finger Lakes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent upper-level disturbance, together with a northward-tracking surface low from the Mid-Atlantic region brought light to moderate snowfall to portions of eastern New York from late April 11 through early afternoon April 12. The heaviest snowfall was concentrated south of Interstate 90 and confined to the higher terrain regions of the Eastern Catskills, Helderbergs, and Taconics. Accumulations of one half inch to 6 inches were common within and south of the Capital Region, with much of this falling during the overnight period of April 11. As this occurred over a weekend, little impact to travel occurred, though slippery road conditions did occur. ||Key impacts: slippery road conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Zone total snowfall of 4.1 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree downed across driveway on US-11.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent upper-level disturbance, together with a northward-tracking surface low from the Mid-Atlantic region brought light to moderate snowfall to portions of eastern New York from late April 11 through early afternoon April 12. The heaviest snowfall was concentrated south of Interstate 90 and confined to the higher terrain regions of the Eastern Catskills, Helderbergs, and Taconics. Accumulations of one half inch to 6 inches were common within and south of the Capital Region, with much of this falling during the overnight period of April 11. As this occurred over a weekend, little impact to travel occurred, though slippery road conditions did occur. ||Key impacts: slippery road conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Zone average snowfall of 2.3 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and evening of the 29th along and ahead of a strong cold front that swept through the area. An increase in moisture, shear and instability during the afternoon and temperatures rising into the upper 70s to lower 80s led to strong to severe thunderstorms developing with damaging winds being the main impact. Thunderstorms started off cellular in nature before ending as a more organized line of showers and storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down trees and wires on Hornby Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front tracking east across the western and north central New York area on August 24th, caused strong to severe thunderstorms to develop. Severe thunderstorms resulted in wind damage and large hail for portions of the area east of the Genesee Valley.  Thunderstorms initially developed over the eastern end of Lake Erie during the morning hours and strengthened and expanded in coverage through the morning and into the afternoon as the cold front and thunderstorms pushed east. Increasing instability and shear ahead of the front, along with the forcing from the front caused the thunderstorms to reach severe limits.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of half of a tree and wires downed across State Route 444.

A strong cold front crossed the area producing scattered showers and isolated thunderstorms. The front moved into an environment that had limited instability yet strong winds aloft. The lower atmosphere was also supportive of mixing down strong damaging wind gusts to the surface as was the case with a few of the showers and thunderstorms crossing the Southern Tier of NY.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds led to multiple reports of trees and wires down in and around Addison.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front and upper-level wave moving across the area triggered scattered showers and thunderstorms during the afternoon and early evening. While wind shear values were modest, there was sufficient instability for a few storms to become strong to severe producing damaging wind gusts, hail, and heavy rainfall. A strong outflow boundary associated with one of the storms also knocked down multiple trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down trees and wires.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dome of high pressure brought a period of very hot and humid conditions across eastern New York from June 22-25, 2025. The heat began across the mid-Hudson Valley and southern Taconics on June 22 before expanding across the rest of eastern New York on June 23-24. High heat became more localized on June 25 mainly occurring across portions of the Hudson Valley. Actual air temperatures rose into the 90s for multiple days across many valley areas with Poughkeepsie reaching 100 degrees on June 24. Little relief from the heat occurred at night. Peak heat index values (or feels-like temperatures) were between 95 and 104 degrees Fahrenheit in many locations. The highest heat index values (feels-like temperatures) occurred on June 23 with values between 105 and 115 degrees Fahrenheit.||Numerous cooling stations were opened during this period of extreme heat. In addition, preliminary data from the U.S. Health and Human Services showed that eight-year records for one day heat-related emergency department visits were set during this heat wave across region 2 which included all of New York state as well as New Jersey. Up to 7000 customers in Bethlehem lost power on June 23, some in part to the heat wave. Other sporadic outages occurred in other locations. An assisted living facility in Bethlehem lost power on June 23 leading in three people needing to be hospitalized, but none of them needed care for heat-related concerns. The extreme heat also prompted several area school districts to modify their schedules for the last week of school. New York Governor Kathy Hochul declared a state of emergency for 32 New York state counties ahead of the heat wave which included Albany, Columbia, Dutchess, Greene, Rensselaer, Saratoga, Schenectady, Ulster, Warren and Washington.

Dangerous levels of heat and humidity preceded a potent upper level shortwave, corresponding jet streak and strong cold front that led to the development of scattered showers and thunderstorms across portions of eastern New York. A few of these thunderstorms reached severe magnitude, particularly in Dutchess and Rensselaer Counties, producing damaging wind gusts that led to tree damage in these areas. Most of the damage reports relayed road closures and blockages due to one or several downed trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public relayed a delayed report of multiple trees down in Millerton via social media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving cold front arrived during the evening hours, moving into a very moist environment with strong wind shear. With some sunshine around during the afternoon ahead of the front, there was some modest instability. A few storms became strong to severe with locally damaging winds and heavy rain. One man was injured after getting struck by lightning while closing windows on his enclosed porch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A man was injured after being struck by lightning while closing windows on an enclosed porch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary coming in from the west moved into a very warm, moist and unstable environment, leading to the development of scattered showers and isolated thunderstorms. Storms started out as cellular in nature before becoming more linear with locally damaging wind gusts and hail being the main impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down a tree onto a road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective system (MCS) dropped southward across Central NY from eastern Ontario, moving into an environment with plenty of moisture and instability during the early morning hours. This complex of thunderstorms started to bow out and produced widespread damaging wind gusts and heavy rain.  A couple of small kinks along the leading edge of the line of storms did result in two EF-1 tornadoes spinning up before daybreak in Oneida County. Unfortunately one of the tornadoes resulted in 3 fatalities in Clark Mills, NY.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down a tree on NY 365 between Mappa Avenue and Mapledale Road blocking the road.

This complex of thunderstorms started to bow out and produced widespread damaging wind gusts and heavy rain.  A couple of small kinks along the leading edge of the line of storms did result in two EF-1 tornadoes spinning up before daybreak in Oneida County. Unfortunately one of the tornadoes resulted in 3 fatalities in Clark Mills, NY.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A deadly pre-dawn tornado spun up at a kink in a line of severe thunderstorms in Clark Mills. Numerous trees were uprooted or snapped, with many of them on structures. Three homes were sliced or crushed by trees. Two residents were killed in one of the heavily-damaged homes, and a third fatality occurred on the crushed end of another residence. The tornado quickly became intense while traveling southwest |initially, and destroyed a historic grist mill along Oriskany Creek. The tornado then became absorbed into the southward motion of the line of thunderstorms, traveling along Kirkland Avenue before ending. While there was this focused corridor of convergent enhanced EF1 tornado damage, other scattered tree damage could be found throughout the area associated with damaging winds up to 70 mph from the line of severe storms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective system (MCS) dropped southward across Central NY from eastern Ontario, moving into an environment with plenty of moisture and instability during the early morning hours. Torrential rainfall accompanied these storms and produced widespread flash flooding and small river flooding. Estimates of 3 to 5 inches, with locally higher amounts, fell mostly within a 6-hour period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flood inundation up to a residence along Geneganstslet Creek was occurring near the intersection of Hurlburt Road and Pike Road.

Actual air temperatures rose into the 90s for multiple days across many valley areas with Poughkeepsie reaching 100 degrees on June 24. Little relief from the heat occurred at night. Peak heat index values (or feels-like temperatures) were between 95 and 104 degrees Fahrenheit in many locations. The highest heat index values (feels-like temperatures) occurred on June 23 with values between 105 and 115 degrees Fahrenheit.||Numerous cooling stations were opened during this period of extreme heat. In addition, preliminary data from the U.S. Health and Human Services showed that eight-year records for one day heat-related emergency department visits were set during this heat wave across region 2 which included all of New York state as well as New Jersey. Up to 7000 customers in Bethlehem lost power on June 23, some in part to the heat wave. Other sporadic outages occurred in other locations. An assisted living facility in Bethlehem lost power on June 23 leading in three people needing to be hospitalized, but none of them needed care for heat-related concerns. The extreme heat also prompted several area school districts to modify their schedules for the last week of school. New York Governor Kathy Hochul declared a state of emergency for 32 New York state counties ahead of the heat wave which included Albany, Columbia, Dutchess, Greene, Rensselaer, Saratoga, Schenectady, Ulster, Warren and Washington.

Dangerous levels of heat and humidity preceded a potent upper level shortwave, corresponding jet streak and strong cold front that led to the development of scattered showers and thunderstorms across portions of eastern New York. A few of these thunderstorms reached severe magnitude, particularly in Dutchess and Rensselaer Counties, producing damaging wind gusts that led to tree damage in these areas. Most of the damage reports relayed road closures and blockages due to one or several downed trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A local 911 call center operator reported a tree down on Gifford Rd.

A mesoscale convective system (MCS) dropped southward across Central NY from eastern Ontario, moving into an environment with plenty of moisture and instability during the early morning hours. This complex of thunderstorms started to bow out and produced widespread damaging wind gusts and heavy rain.  A couple of small kinks along the leading edge of the line of storms did result in two EF-1 tornadoes spinning up before daybreak in Oneida County. Unfortunately one of the tornadoes resulted in 3 fatalities in Clark Mills, NY.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked wires down on John Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective system (MCS) dropped southward across Central NY from eastern Ontario, moving into an environment with plenty of moisture and instability during the early morning hours. A couple of small kinks along the leading edge of the line of storms did result in two EF-1 tornadoes spinning up before daybreak in Oneida County. Unfortunately one of the tornadoes resulted in 3 fatalities in Clark Mills, NY.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A pre-dawn line of severe thunderstorms dropped south across the county. Rotation occurred in a kink in the line of storms, generating an EF1 tornado from just south of Westmoreland, through the western edge of the Hamilton College Campus, to near|Clinton. Numerous trees were uprooted or snapped, and a farm sustained significant damage to its silo and outbuildings. Debris from the farm was lofted at least 1000 feet across the field, with some of it impaling the ground as debris missiles. While there was this focused corridor of convergent enhanced EF1 tornado damage, other scattered tree damage could be found throughout the area associated with damaging winds up to 70 mph from the line of severe storms.

A couple of small kinks along the leading edge of the line of storms did result in two EF-1 tornadoes spinning up before daybreak in Oneida County. Unfortunately one of the tornadoes resulted in 3 fatalities in Clark Mills, NY.

Low pressure moving along a stationary front positioned over South Central New York triggered slow-moving, heavy rain-producing thunderstorms during the early morning hours. Pockets of 2 to 3 inches of rain fell over just a few hours causing urban and small stream flash flooding along with road and bridge washouts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water and debris was reported across the road south of the Town of Hartwick.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective system (MCS) dropped southward across Central NY from eastern Ontario, moving into an environment with plenty of moisture and instability during the early morning hours. Torrential rainfall accompanied these storms and produced widespread flash flooding and small river flooding. Estimates of 3 to 5 inches, with locally higher amounts, fell mostly within a 6-hour period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous roads, bridges, and homes were flooded in the city.  Ten people were stranded by flood waters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dome of high pressure brought a period of very hot and humid conditions across eastern New York from June 22-25, 2025. The heat began across the mid-Hudson Valley and southern Taconics on June 22 before expanding across the rest of eastern New York on June 23-24. High heat became more localized on June 25 mainly occurring across portions of the Hudson Valley. Actual air temperatures rose into the 90s for multiple days across many valley areas with Poughkeepsie reaching 100 degrees on June 24. Little relief from the heat occurred at night. Peak heat index values (or feels-like temperatures) were between 95 and 104 degrees Fahrenheit in many locations. The highest heat index values (feels-like temperatures) occurred on June 23 with values between 105 and 115 degrees Fahrenheit.||Numerous cooling stations were opened during this period of extreme heat. In addition, preliminary data from the U.S. Health and Human Services showed that eight-year records for one day heat-related emergency department visits were set during this heat wave across region 2 which included all of New York state as well as New Jersey. Up to 7000 customers in Bethlehem lost power on June 23, some in part to the heat wave. Other sporadic outages occurred in other locations. An assisted living facility in Bethlehem lost power on June 23 leading in three people needing to be hospitalized, but none of them needed care for heat-related concerns. The extreme heat also prompted several area school districts to modify their schedules for the last week of school. New York Governor Kathy Hochul declared a state of emergency for 32 New York state counties ahead of the heat wave which included Albany, Columbia, Dutchess, Greene, Rensselaer, Saratoga, Schenectady, Ulster, Warren and Washington.

The passage of a strong cold front led to windy conditions, especially across portions of eastern New York's higher terrain regions. Wind gusts anywhere from 20 to 40 mph were common across all of eastern New York, however, with a peak gust of 42 mph recorded by a CWOP site in Kerhonkson in Ulster County. Additionally, an isolated report of wind damage was relayed to NWS Albany from the Department of Highways via NY Alert wherein NY Route 343 was closed on both sides due to a downed tree.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A New York alert message from the Department of Highways relayed the closure of both lanes of NY route 343 due to a large downed tree just past Old Route 22.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front and upper-level wave moving across the area triggered scattered showers and thunderstorms during the afternoon and early evening. While wind shear values were modest, there was sufficient instability for a few storms to become strong to severe producing damaging wind gusts, hail, and heavy rainfall. A strong outflow boundary associated with one of the storms also knocked down multiple trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds resulted in multiple reports of trees down near West Main Street.

The extreme heat also prompted several area school districts to modify their schedules for the last week of school. New York Governor Kathy Hochul declared a state of emergency for 32 New York state counties ahead of the heat wave which included Albany, Columbia, Dutchess, Greene, Rensselaer, Saratoga, Schenectady, Ulster, Warren and Washington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective system (MCS) dropped southward across Central NY from eastern Ontario, moving into an environment with plenty of moisture and instability during the early morning hours. Torrential rainfall accompanied these storms and produced widespread flash flooding and small river flooding. Estimates of 3 to 5 inches, with locally higher amounts, fell mostly within a 6-hour period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding occurred on NY 5 in both directions between Route 233 and Limberlost Road. All lanes were blocked.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The road was closed along Route 30 near Hubbell Corners Road due to flooding.

A cold front and upper-level wave moving across the area triggered scattered showers and thunderstorms during the afternoon and early evening. While wind shear values were modest, there was sufficient instability for a few storms to become strong to severe producing damaging wind gusts, hail, and heavy rainfall. A strong outflow boundary associated with one of the storms also knocked down multiple trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down a tree on Town Line Road.

A slow-moving cold front arrived during the evening hours, moving into a very moist environment with strong wind shear. With some sunshine around during the afternoon ahead of the front, there was some modest instability. A few storms became strong to severe with locally damaging winds and heavy rain. One man was injured after getting struck by lightning while closing windows on his enclosed porch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down a tree onto a road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was occurring on roadways throughout Auburn.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front crossed the area during the afternoon of Thursday, the 13th, and a strong flow from the west-northwest occurred in its wake. Lake effect snow developed downwind of Lake Ontario Thursday night and continued into Friday before starting to lift off to the north Friday afternoon and evening. With a multi-lake connection developing and mostly uniform winds at the lower levels late Thursday evening into early Friday, heavy snow fell at times over Oneida, Madison and Onondaga counties. Winds gusted between 30 and 45 mph at times leading to areas of blowing and drifting snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake effect snow accumulated between 5 and 13 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dome of high pressure brought a period of very hot and humid conditions across eastern New York from June 22-25, 2025. The heat began across the mid-Hudson Valley and southern Taconics on June 22 before expanding across the rest of eastern New York on June 23-24. High heat became more localized on June 25 mainly occurring across portions of the Hudson Valley. Actual air temperatures rose into the 90s for multiple days across many valley areas with Poughkeepsie reaching 100 degrees on June 24. Little relief from the heat occurred at night. Peak heat index values (or feels-like temperatures) were between 95 and 104 degrees Fahrenheit in many locations. The highest heat index values (feels-like temperatures) occurred on June 23 with values between 105 and 115 degrees Fahrenheit.||Numerous cooling stations were opened during this period of extreme heat. In addition, preliminary data from the U.S. Health and Human Services showed that eight-year records for one day heat-related emergency department visits were set during this heat wave across region 2 which included all of New York state as well as New Jersey. Up to 7000 customers in Bethlehem lost power on June 23, some in part to the heat wave. Other sporadic outages occurred in other locations. An assisted living facility in Bethlehem lost power on June 23 leading in three people needing to be hospitalized, but none of them needed care for heat-related concerns. The extreme heat also prompted several area school districts to modify their schedules for the last week of school. New York Governor Kathy Hochul declared a state of emergency for 32 New York state counties ahead of the heat wave which included Albany, Columbia, Dutchess, Greene, Rensselaer, Saratoga, Schenectady, Ulster, Warren and Washington.

A mesoscale convective system (MCS) dropped southward across Central NY from eastern Ontario, moving into an environment with plenty of moisture and instability during the early morning hours. This complex of thunderstorms started to bow out and produced widespread damaging wind gusts and heavy rain.  A couple of small kinks along the leading edge of the line of storms did result in two EF-1 tornadoes spinning up before daybreak in Oneida County. Unfortunately one of the tornadoes resulted in 3 fatalities in Clark Mills, NY.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down trees along a property line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms developed in a warm and humid airmass during the afternoon hours over the Southern Tier of NY. Rainfall rates were excessive and produced up to 3 inches of rain within a 1-to-2-hour time span. This led to areas of minor flash flooding, mostly confined to street and poor drainage areas in developed areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported on Avenue of Oaks blocking intersection with Sharps Hill Road.

Estimates of 3 to 5 inches, with locally higher amounts, fell mostly within a 6-hour period.

A cold front and upper-level wave moving across the area triggered scattered showers and thunderstorms during the afternoon and early evening. While wind shear values were modest, there was sufficient instability for a few storms to become strong to severe producing damaging wind gusts, hail, and heavy rainfall. A strong outflow boundary associated with one of the storms also knocked down multiple trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down a tree on Circle Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong ridge of high pressure over the region led to the development of record-breaking heat and humidity on this day. Heat index values approached 110 degrees in urban valley areas, which resulted in extreme heat warnings to be issued. This was the second day in a multi-day heat wave event. Unfortunately a woman suffered a medical emergency in the late afternoon due to the heat and passed away at her apartment.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: During the late afternoon of the second day of a multi-day heatwave, a 65 year old woman on the 3rd/top floor of an apartment building in the Village of Owego suffered a medical emergency due to the heat and passed away. The apartment had no air conditioning and had warmed to 105 degrees. Even emergency responses were hindered due to extreme heat conditions causing the providers to become fatigued at a faster-than-normal rate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On March 16, a strong area of low pressure tracked across the Central Great Lakes into southeast Ontario and southwest Quebec. The warm front associated with the system tracked north across the western New York area during the morning hours and an associated low level jet increased winds out of the south, especially for downslope areas near Lake Erie. This resulted in non thunderstorm winds that caused damage across much of Chautauqua County prior to the arrival of severe thunderstorms during the early afternoon. Wind Advisories or Warnings were in effect for all of the western and north central New York counties due to the potential for gusty winds. Convection developed across Ontario and Pennsylvania during the late morning hours along a pre-frontal trough associated with the system. As the pre-frontal trough and early convection tracked into and then across the western New York area, conditions were favorable for strong to severe thunderstorms to develop. Severe thunderstorms caused damage across much of the Buffalo forecast area, mainly inland from Lake Erie and extending to the North Country. ||In addition to the damage noted above across Chautauqua County, a measured wind gust of 66 MPH occurred at the Dunkirk Airport during the morning of March 16.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree downed on car on Ridge Road in Williamson.

Actual air temperatures rose into the 90s for multiple days across many valley areas with Poughkeepsie reaching 100 degrees on June 24. Little relief from the heat occurred at night. Peak heat index values (or feels-like temperatures) were between 95 and 104 degrees Fahrenheit in many locations. The highest heat index values (feels-like temperatures) occurred on June 23 with values between 105 and 115 degrees Fahrenheit.||Numerous cooling stations were opened during this period of extreme heat. In addition, preliminary data from the U.S. Health and Human Services showed that eight-year records for one day heat-related emergency department visits were set during this heat wave across region 2 which included all of New York state as well as New Jersey. Up to 7000 customers in Bethlehem lost power on June 23, some in part to the heat wave. Other sporadic outages occurred in other locations. An assisted living facility in Bethlehem lost power on June 23 leading in three people needing to be hospitalized, but none of them needed care for heat-related concerns. The extreme heat also prompted several area school districts to modify their schedules for the last week of school. New York Governor Kathy Hochul declared a state of emergency for 32 New York state counties ahead of the heat wave which included Albany, Columbia, Dutchess, Greene, Rensselaer, Saratoga, Schenectady, Ulster, Warren and Washington.

A cold front and upper-level wave moving across the area triggered scattered showers and thunderstorms during the afternoon and early evening. While wind shear values were modest, there was sufficient instability for a few storms to become strong to severe producing damaging wind gusts, hail, and heavy rainfall. A strong outflow boundary associated with one of the storms also knocked down multiple trees.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down a tree onto a roadway near County Road 129 and Hayts Corners East Road.

A slow-moving cold front arrived during the evening hours, moving into a very moist environment with strong wind shear. With some sunshine around during the afternoon ahead of the front, there was some modest instability. A few storms became strong to severe with locally damaging winds and heavy rain. One man was injured after getting struck by lightning while closing windows on his enclosed porch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds uprooted a large tree in a yard, and the tree landed on a dog kennel.

A mesoscale convective system (MCS) dropped southward across Central NY from eastern Ontario, moving into an environment with plenty of moisture and instability during the early morning hours. This complex of thunderstorms started to bow out and produced widespread damaging wind gusts and heavy rain.  A couple of small kinks along the leading edge of the line of storms did result in two EF-1 tornadoes spinning up before daybreak in Oneida County. Unfortunately one of the tornadoes resulted in 3 fatalities in Clark Mills, NY.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down a tree across NY 46 between Jackson Hill Road and Homes Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective system (MCS) dropped southward across Central NY from eastern Ontario, moving into an environment with plenty of moisture and instability during the early morning hours. Torrential rainfall accompanied these storms and produced widespread flash flooding and small river flooding. Estimates of 3 to 5 inches, with locally higher amounts, fell mostly within a 6-hour period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NY 80 closed in both directions due to flooding. Town officials report that three quarters of the roadway is washed out at the intersection with Davis Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moving along a stationary front positioned over South Central New York triggered slow-moving, heavy rain-producing thunderstorms during the early morning hours. Pockets of 2 to 3 inches of rain fell over just a few hours causing urban and small stream flash flooding along with road and bridge washouts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A police officer reported a road washout with water and debris on Health Camp Road.

Actual air temperatures rose into the 90s for multiple days across many valley areas with Poughkeepsie reaching 100 degrees on June 24. Little relief from the heat occurred at night. Peak heat index values (or feels-like temperatures) were between 95 and 104 degrees Fahrenheit in many locations. The highest heat index values (feels-like temperatures) occurred on June 23 with values between 105 and 115 degrees Fahrenheit.||Numerous cooling stations were opened during this period of extreme heat. In addition, preliminary data from the U.S. Health and Human Services showed that eight-year records for one day heat-related emergency department visits were set during this heat wave across region 2 which included all of New York state as well as New Jersey. Up to 7000 customers in Bethlehem lost power on June 23, some in part to the heat wave. Other sporadic outages occurred in other locations. An assisted living facility in Bethlehem lost power on June 23 leading in three people needing to be hospitalized, but none of them needed care for heat-related concerns. The extreme heat also prompted several area school districts to modify their schedules for the last week of school. New York Governor Kathy Hochul declared a state of emergency for 32 New York state counties ahead of the heat wave which included Albany, Columbia, Dutchess, Greene, Rensselaer, Saratoga, Schenectady, Ulster, Warren and Washington.

A 59-year-old South Carolina man drowned on the afternoon of August 23rd while swimming at Sailors Haven on Fire Island. A rip current statement was in effect.  4 to 6 ft of breaking surf and dangerous rip currents were observed by lifeguards at our ocean beaches as Hurricane Erin departed thru the North Atlantic.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 59-year-old South Carolina man drowned Saturday afternoon August 23rd  while swimming at Sailors Haven on Fire Island. Police said Ishmoile Mohammed became caught in a rip current that pulled him west. Lifeguards brought him to shore about 2:45 p.m. and, with assistance from Fire Island National Seashore staff, tried to save him.||Mohammed, a section chief at New York Army National Guard, was airlifted to South Shore University Hospital in Bay Shore, where he was pronounced dead.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex winter storm impacted eastern New York between February 15th and 16th, 2025 bringing snow, freezing rain, and strong winds. The greatest accumulations of snow fell at the highest elevations of the Southwest Adirondacks, Eastern Catskills, Helderbergs, and Taconics with a range of around 5 to 9 inches. Accumulations elsewhere ranged from about 1 to 4 inches. Most of the region also saw freezing rain, though the highest amounts of flat ice accreted within the Mohawk and Hudson Valleys as well as the Eastern Catskills. In these areas, generally around 0.15 to 0.35 inches of flat ice were reported. Elsewhere, anywhere from 0.05 to around 0.14 was common. At the event's conclusion, the total liquid equivalent across eastern New York from both snow and freezing rain ranged from about 0.9 to 1.2 inches. The combination of heavy wet snow, heavy ice, and gusty winds led to significant power outages across the region, in addition to numerous motor vehicle accidents, road closures, and subsequent travel delays. ||Key Impacts: power outages, motor vehicle accidents, travel delays, road closures, county-based states of emergency.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mixed precipitation winter storm led to the combination of zone average snowfall of 7.7 inches, zone average flat ice accumulation of 0.21 inches, and zone average liquid equivalent of 1.06 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Immediately on the heels of a complex winter storm that impacted eastern New York on February 15-16th, 2025, a prolonged lake effect event began with regionwide strong to high winds and bitter cold in the wake of the storm. Lake effect bands became significant off Lake Ontario, extending as far south and east as the Capital District and dumping heavy amounts of snow in Herkimer, Hamilton, Fulton, and Montgomery Counties. Reports of near zero visibility, due to the combination of heavy snow and strong winds inducing blowing snow, came in from emergency managers of Montgomery County who later declared a state of emergency from 4 PM on February 17th through 4 PM on February 18th. Here, the greatest amount of snowfall was about 7 inches near the town of Amsterdam. In Fulton County, while there was no state of emergency declared, 31 motor vehicle accidents were reported, two of which were noted to have sustained injuries. Strongest wind gusts throughout eastern New York ranged from around 40 mph to 55 mph with a peak wind gust of 58 mph recorded in Saratoga Springs. The cold airmass inflicted across the region on the departing side of the storm of the days prior led to wind chill values anywhere from -1 to -20 with the lowest wind chills noted within the Southwest Adirondacks and Eastern Catskills. ||Key Impacts: road closures, travel delays, states of emergency, motor-vehicle accidents.

Strong to severe thunderstorms developed along a cold front and within the warm sector of an approaching system. Favorable shear, along with forcing from the cold front helped to develop severe storms that also resulted in a confirmed tornado in the western Southern Tier. Additionally, a very moist atmosphere was present within the systems warm sector, helping to contribute to heavy rainfall with showers and thunderstorms. Some of the heavy rain that developed across the western Southern Tier, combined with antecedent conditions caused flash flooding in Allegany County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees down in Steamburg, including tree a on a house.

Intensifying low pressure across Maine and New Brunswick combined with high pressure across Ontario and a cooler, drier airmass across NY and VT led to strong mixing of the atmosphere and gusty gradient winds of 35 to 40 mph with a few higher gusts.||Unfortunately, winds knocked down a large leader branch of a diseased tree along Lakeshore Drive in Chazy falling onto a moving vehicle that killed the sole occupant.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gusty winds around 40 mph caused a large leader of a diseased tree to fall onto a moving vehicle on Lakeshore Road in Chazy. The sole occupant perished in this incident.

A weak trough across the northern tier of NY and VT provided enough lift in a very hot and humid airmass to produce scattered thunderstorms with a few isolated pulse severe thunderstorms due to localized downburst.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees and many branches downed by thunderstorm winds in the West Potsdam vicinity, including Baghdad Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Immediately on the heels of a complex winter storm that impacted eastern New York on February 15-16th, 2025, a prolonged lake effect event began with regionwide strong to high winds and bitter cold in the wake of the storm. Lake effect bands became significant off Lake Ontario, extending as far south and east as the Capital District and dumping heavy amounts of snow in Herkimer, Hamilton, Fulton, and Montgomery Counties. Reports of near zero visibility, due to the combination of heavy snow and strong winds inducing blowing snow, came in from emergency managers of Montgomery County who later declared a state of emergency from 4 PM on February 17th through 4 PM on February 18th. Here, the greatest amount of snowfall was about 7 inches near the town of Amsterdam. In Fulton County, while there was no state of emergency declared, 31 motor vehicle accidents were reported, two of which were noted to have sustained injuries. Strongest wind gusts throughout eastern New York ranged from around 40 mph to 55 mph with a peak wind gust of 58 mph recorded in Saratoga Springs. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex winter storm impacted eastern New York between February 15th and 16th, 2025 bringing snow, freezing rain, and strong winds. The greatest accumulations of snow fell at the highest elevations of the Southwest Adirondacks, Eastern Catskills, Helderbergs, and Taconics with a range of around 5 to 9 inches. Accumulations elsewhere ranged from about 1 to 4 inches. Most of the region also saw freezing rain, though the highest amounts of flat ice accreted within the Mohawk and Hudson Valleys as well as the Eastern Catskills. In these areas, generally around 0.15 to 0.35 inches of flat ice were reported. Elsewhere, anywhere from 0.05 to around 0.14 was common. At the event's conclusion, the total liquid equivalent across eastern New York from both snow and freezing rain ranged from about 0.9 to 1.2 inches. The combination of heavy wet snow, heavy ice, and gusty winds led to significant power outages across the region, in addition to numerous motor vehicle accidents, road closures, and subsequent travel delays. ||Key Impacts: power outages, motor vehicle accidents, travel delays, road closures, county-based states of emergency.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mixed precipitation event led to the combination of zone average snowfall of 2.9 inches, zone average flat ice accumulation of 0.20 inches, and zone average liquid equivalent of 0.97 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dangerous heat across the region for both June 23rd and 24th with daytime temperatures in the mid to upper 90s and dewpoints in the 70s creating heat index values between 100 and 110.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal trough approached at the surface out ahead of a cold front. Aloft, a shortwave trough led to falling heights into the afternoon. These features combined with MLCAPE values between 1000 and 2000 J/kg and moderate shear allowed for thunderstorms to develop and become organized. These storms produced damaging wind gusts and frequent cloud to ground lightning in southeast NY.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree down blocking road on Clubhouse Road in Merrick NY.

Reports of near zero visibility, due to the combination of heavy snow and strong winds inducing blowing snow, came in from emergency managers of Montgomery County who later declared a state of emergency from 4 PM on February 17th through 4 PM on February 18th. Here, the greatest amount of snowfall was about 7 inches near the town of Amsterdam. In Fulton County, while there was no state of emergency declared, 31 motor vehicle accidents were reported, two of which were noted to have sustained injuries. Strongest wind gusts throughout eastern New York ranged from around 40 mph to 55 mph with a peak wind gust of 58 mph recorded in Saratoga Springs. The cold airmass inflicted across the region on the departing side of the storm of the days prior led to wind chill values anywhere from -1 to -20 with the lowest wind chills noted within the Southwest Adirondacks and Eastern Catskills. ||Key Impacts: road closures, travel delays, states of emergency, motor-vehicle accidents.

An upper level area of high pressure, this time of the year called a heat dome, was centered across the Appalachians/Mid-Atlantic states ridging across the northeast including NY and New England.||This airmass on June 23rd was considerably hot and humid with temperatures in the 90s and oppressive dewpoints in the low to mid 70s. Heat index values ranged from the mid 90s across the Adirondacks to the 100 to 110 range across the St. Lawrence and Champlain valleys.||Temperatures were only a few degrees lower for most of NY on June 24th but still  dangerous hot with heat index values mainly between 95 and 105. Health related impacts were not available.||Here were temperature records for the three recognized climatological sites (Plattsburgh, Saranac Lake and Massena) in northern NY.||June 23rd:|Plattsburgh - 101 degrees, surpassing the daily record and TIED the ALL-TIME Warmest Temperature.|Saranac Lake - 90 degrees surpassing the daily record.|Massena - 92 degrees TIED the daily record.||June 24th:|Plattsburgh - 99 degrees, surpassing the daily record.|Saranac Lake - 90 degrees TIED the daily record.|Massena - 93 degrees surpassing the daily record.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dangerous heat across the region for both June 23rd and 24th with daytime temperatures in the lower to mid 90s and dewpoints in the 70s creating heat index values between 95 and 105 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex winter storm impacted eastern New York between February 15th and 16th, 2025 bringing snow, freezing rain, and strong winds. The greatest accumulations of snow fell at the highest elevations of the Southwest Adirondacks, Eastern Catskills, Helderbergs, and Taconics with a range of around 5 to 9 inches. Accumulations elsewhere ranged from about 1 to 4 inches. Most of the region also saw freezing rain, though the highest amounts of flat ice accreted within the Mohawk and Hudson Valleys as well as the Eastern Catskills. In these areas, generally around 0.15 to 0.35 inches of flat ice were reported. Elsewhere, anywhere from 0.05 to around 0.14 was common. At the event's conclusion, the total liquid equivalent across eastern New York from both snow and freezing rain ranged from about 0.9 to 1.2 inches. The combination of heavy wet snow, heavy ice, and gusty winds led to significant power outages across the region, in addition to numerous motor vehicle accidents, road closures, and subsequent travel delays. ||Key Impacts: power outages, motor vehicle accidents, travel delays, road closures, county-based states of emergency.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mixed precipitation event led to the combination of zone average snowfall of 2.7 inches, zone average flat ice accumulation of 0.32 inches, and zone average liquid equivalent of 1.16 inches.

Immediately on the heels of a complex winter storm that impacted eastern New York on February 15-16th, 2025, a prolonged lake effect event began with regionwide strong to high winds and bitter cold in the wake of the storm. Lake effect bands became significant off Lake Ontario, extending as far south and east as the Capital District and dumping heavy amounts of snow in Herkimer, Hamilton, Fulton, and Montgomery Counties. Reports of near zero visibility, due to the combination of heavy snow and strong winds inducing blowing snow, came in from emergency managers of Montgomery County who later declared a state of emergency from 4 PM on February 17th through 4 PM on February 18th. Here, the greatest amount of snowfall was about 7 inches near the town of Amsterdam. In Fulton County, while there was no state of emergency declared, 31 motor vehicle accidents were reported, two of which were noted to have sustained injuries. Strongest wind gusts throughout eastern New York ranged from around 40 mph to 55 mph with a peak wind gust of 58 mph recorded in Saratoga Springs. The cold airmass inflicted across the region on the departing side of the storm of the days prior led to wind chill values anywhere from -1 to -20 with the lowest wind chills noted within the Southwest Adirondacks and Eastern Catskills. ||Key Impacts: road closures, travel delays, states of emergency, motor-vehicle accidents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep layered troughing approached the area from the west along with a cold front at the surface. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough and associated cold front approached the area. Ahead of these features, the area destabilized to about 2000 J/kg of MLCAPE. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree down on car on Tompkins Avenue at Fingerboard Avenue. The car was not  occupied.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A period of hot and humid weather occurred across portions of eastern New York from July 6-8, 2025 with heat index values (feels like temperatures) reaching 95 to 102 degrees each afternoon across some valley areas. A southward advancing cold front on July 8 brought relief from the heat with the highest values only occurring across the mid-Hudson Valley on this day.

A few showers and thunderstorms developed within the vicinity of a frontal boundary during the afternoon and early evening hours on July 10, 2025. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed and across Old State Road North in the Town of White Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed along lake breeze boundaries and a passing cold front on the afternoon of July 7. While there was plenty of instability, shear values were lower, but just enough to support a few severe thunderstorms that produced damage along with a tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The National Weather Service surveyed damage in the Town of |Phelps in Ontario County from July 7 today. Damage began west of|Melvin Hill Road with a few broken hardwood branches but quickly|intensified to the east-southeast as multiple softwood trees|were snapped and uprooted around two homes and a barn. While|none of those structures received notable damage, multiple trees |nearby saw their trunks snapped. Damage continued east-southeast|to the north of County Road 23 with dozens of hardwood trees |snapped on the north side of a large farm area. As the damage|continued to the east-southeast, three residences received damage|around the roundabout at the intersection of County Road 23 and|Fort Hill Road. The worse damage was located just northwest of|the roundabout, where an entire roof and portions of the wall of|a home were destroyed. Nearby, another roof was fully destroyed,|as well. A barn just south of the roundabout was shifted off its|foundation, and a silo saw its roof about half removed. ||From the damage surveyed today, as well as multiple pictures, |videos, and drone footage shared from multiple sources, the|National Weather Service storm survey team determined the|damage to be consistent with a tornado rated an EF-2 on the|Enhanced Fujita Scale. Maximum winds were estimated at 115 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a frontal boundary moving north of the area and a weak upper level shortwave allowed showers and thunderstorms to spark to our south and west.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A house was struck by lightning causing a fire with moderate damage to roof.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: At the surface, a cold front slowly approached from the west, with a pre-frontal trough developing ahead of it. Aloft, the area remained generally on the eastern edge of mid/upper level troughing. While shear was not all that impressive (about 25 kt of 0-6km bulk shear), strong heating aided in MLCAPE values reaching about 2000 j/kg. With lift from the aforementioned features, and likely help from the right entrance region of an upper level jet streak, showers and thunderstorms started to develop. Some storms reached severe criteria and produce damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree down on the exit 16 ramp of the Palisades Interstate Parkway.

Hot and humid conditions continued across portions of eastern New York on July 25, 2025 ahead of a passing cold front. Peak heat index values (feels like temperatures) between 95 and 102 degrees were observed mainly near and south of the Capital District. In addition, scattered showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of the cold front during the afternoon hours with one storm becoming severe downing trees and wires along its path across Columbia and Dutchess counties.

Peak heat index values (feels like temperatures) between 95 and 102 degrees were observed mainly near and south of the Capital District. In addition, scattered showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of the cold front during the afternoon hours with one storm becoming severe downing trees and wires along its path across Columbia and Dutchess counties.

A cold front passed through with scattered storms, some of which became severe with large hail and damaging winds. Despite earlier showers, the atmosphere still became unstable later in the morning through early afternoon. In addition, high wind shear and steep temperature lapse rates made conditions favorable for severe weather. ||Hail of 1 to 2 inches in diameter was observed from the Finger Lakes and south into New York's Southern Tier. One of the storms passed over the NWS Binghamton Forecast Office and dropped large hail on vehicles parked in the lot while also knocking down trees behind the building. A quick survey by a NWS Binghamton employee found trees stripped of their leaves and some crop damage in the Binghamton area. Due to the extensive area of hail reports and the size of the hail, the full extent of the damage to buildings, vehicles and crops is unknown and the estimated damage costs are likely underdone due to this.  In addition, the severe storms also had strong winds. There were numerous other reports for downed trees and wires. In a few cases, trees fell on cars, houses, or other buildings and often resulted in damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down a tree on Amabel Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms formed along a passing cold front during the early afternoon hours on July 3. Instability and shear supported a few strong to severe thunderstorms during this time, while the cold front provided the forcing to initiate the storm development. Storms formed in the area of the central Genesee Valley and tracked southeast into the Finger Lakes. Severe thunderstorm related reports were limited to one report each of hail and damage, but as the storms continued to track southeast out of the Buffalo forecast area, the storms strengthened further.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail reported on Conesus Lake via trained spotter.

The hail accumulated in some areas making the ground white and roads slippery.

In addition, high wind shear and steep temperature lapse rates made conditions favorable for severe weather. ||Hail of 1 to 2 inches in diameter was observed from the Finger Lakes and south into New York's Southern Tier. One of the storms passed over the NWS Binghamton Forecast Office and dropped large hail on vehicles parked in the lot while also knocking down trees behind the building. A quick survey by a NWS Binghamton employee found trees stripped of their leaves and some crop damage in the Binghamton area. Due to the extensive area of hail reports and the size of the hail, the full extent of the damage to buildings, vehicles and crops is unknown and the estimated damage costs are likely underdone due to this.  In addition, the severe storms also had strong winds. There were numerous other reports for downed trees and wires. In a few cases, trees fell on cars, houses, or other buildings and often resulted in damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down wires over Airport Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A period of hot and humid weather occurred across portions of eastern New York from July 6-8, 2025 with heat index values (feels like temperatures) reaching 95 to 102 degrees each afternoon across some valley areas. A southward advancing cold front on July 8 brought relief from the heat with the highest values only occurring across the mid-Hudson Valley on this day.

A few showers and thunderstorms developed within the vicinity of a frontal boundary during the afternoon and early evening hours on July 10, 2025. A few of these storms became severe downing trees and wires. Most of the damage was concentrated within Saratoga and Washington counties. A 62 mph wind gust was recorded at the Floyd Bennett Memorial Airport near Glens Falls.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees and wires were downed in the Town of Fort Ann including on Pilot Knob Road, Copeland Pond Road, Michigan Lane, Buttermilk Falls Road, Sly Pond Road and Hadlock Pond Road. One of the downed wires on Copeland Pond Road fell on a structure.

A cold front passed through with scattered storms, some of which became severe with large hail and damaging winds. Despite earlier showers, the atmosphere still became unstable later in the morning through early afternoon. In addition, high wind shear and steep temperature lapse rates made conditions favorable for severe weather. ||Hail of 1 to 2 inches in diameter was observed from the Finger Lakes and south into New York's Southern Tier. One of the storms passed over the NWS Binghamton Forecast Office and dropped large hail on vehicles parked in the lot while also knocking down trees behind the building. A quick survey by a NWS Binghamton employee found trees stripped of their leaves and some crop damage in the Binghamton area. Due to the extensive area of hail reports and the size of the hail, the full extent of the damage to buildings, vehicles and crops is unknown and the estimated damage costs are likely underdone due to this.  In addition, the severe storms also had strong winds. There were numerous other reports for downed trees and wires. In a few cases, trees fell on cars, houses, or other buildings and often resulted in damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated half dollar hail via social media photo.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds snapped several poles on Muckey Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms were supported by an unstable environment. Shear was weak, so most storms stayed sub-severe. However, one storm did become severe as it moved across the Mohawk Valley and Tug Hill Plateau. Strong gusts with this storm knocked down a tree onto a roadway. Otherwise, most reports this day were related to heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down a tree on road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front slowly moved across northern NY during the afternoon and evening hours of July 13th. Several rounds of tropical showers and thunderstorms traversed the area with a few storms pulsing to severe limits. In addition, localized heavy rainfall caused flash flooding in the form of a landslide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large landslide/debris flow occurred in the Avalanche Pass/Avalanche Lake area, blocking a popular hiking trail and clogging the upper portions of Avalanche Lake with heavy debris and timber.

One of the storms passed over the NWS Binghamton Forecast Office and dropped large hail on vehicles parked in the lot while also knocking down trees behind the building. A quick survey by a NWS Binghamton employee found trees stripped of their leaves and some crop damage in the Binghamton area. Due to the extensive area of hail reports and the size of the hail, the full extent of the damage to buildings, vehicles and crops is unknown and the estimated damage costs are likely underdone due to this.  In addition, the severe storms also had strong winds. There were numerous other reports for downed trees and wires. In a few cases, trees fell on cars, houses, or other buildings and often resulted in damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down numerous trees on Upper Front Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm and unstable environment ahead of an approaching cold front supported the development of scattered thunderstorms. Weak steering winds contributed to slow moving storms repeating over the same areas which contributed to areas of heavy rainfall and flash flooding. Several locations around central New York reported flooded roads along with washouts of culverts and small bridges.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Caldwell Hill Road was closed due to a washout.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak frontal system moving through a warm and excessively moist environment triggered thunderstorms with torrential rainfall. These storms were slow moving and produced copious amounts of rain which led to localized flash flooding of roads, small streams and a few residences.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was occurring along Prospect Valley Road with water pouring into a residence. The garage and basement of the residence was flooded and a shed was shifted onto another property.

A few showers and thunderstorms developed within the vicinity of a frontal boundary during the afternoon and early evening hours on July 10, 2025. A few of these storms became severe downing trees and wires. Most of the damage was concentrated within Saratoga and Washington counties. A 62 mph wind gust was recorded at the Floyd Bennett Memorial Airport near Glens Falls.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A report was received on social media of a tree down on a fence along Liz Ann Drive near Saratoga Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough and associated cold front approached the area. Ahead of these features, the area destabilized to about 2000 J/kg of MLCAPE. Storms fired up along a pre-frontal trough and with the help of about 30 knots of Effective Bulk Shear several storms were able to become severe and produce damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree down on car on Fremont Avenue in Grant City.

A few showers and thunderstorms developed within the vicinity of a frontal boundary during the afternoon and early evening hours on July 10, 2025. A few of these storms became severe downing trees and wires. Most of the damage was concentrated within Saratoga and Washington counties. A 62 mph wind gust was recorded at the Floyd Bennett Memorial Airport near Glens Falls.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was down on wires along Meeting House Road in the Town of Easton.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to quarter size was reported at Glass Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of an approaching cold front on July 20, 2025. A few storms became severe downing trees and wires across eastern New York.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple of trees were downed near Charleston Four Corners.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front passed through with scattered storms, some of which became severe with large hail and damaging winds. Despite earlier showers, the atmosphere still became unstable later in the morning through early afternoon. In addition, high wind shear and steep temperature lapse rates made conditions favorable for severe weather. ||Hail of 1 to 2 inches in diameter was observed from the Finger Lakes and south into New York's Southern Tier. One of the storms passed over the NWS Binghamton Forecast Office and dropped large hail on vehicles parked in the lot while also knocking down trees behind the building. A quick survey by a NWS Binghamton employee found trees stripped of their leaves and some crop damage in the Binghamton area. Due to the extensive area of hail reports and the size of the hail, the full extent of the damage to buildings, vehicles and crops is unknown and the estimated damage costs are likely underdone due to this.  In addition, the severe storms also had strong winds. There were numerous other reports for downed trees and wires. In a few cases, trees fell on cars, houses, or other buildings and often resulted in damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down multiple trees and wires.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm and unstable environment ahead of an approaching cold front supported the development of scattered thunderstorms. Weak steering winds contributed to slow moving storms repeating over the same areas which contributed to areas of heavy rainfall and flash flooding. Several locations around central New York reported flooded roads along with washouts of culverts and small bridges.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route 26 was closed due to flooding.
