EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak frontal system moving through a warm and excessively moist environment triggered thunderstorms with torrential rainfall. These storms were slow moving and produced copious amounts of rain which led to localized flash flooding of roads, small streams and a few residences.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roadway flooding was occurring at the intersection of Old Route 76 and Yaple Rd. Reports of water and debris across the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system moving through the lower Great Lakes region triggered isolated thunderstorms over the Central Southern Tier of NY. A saturated atmosphere helped to support torrential rainfall over Steuben county which led to some flooding and washouts of local roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding of several roads and streets was reported around the intersection of State Route 36 and County Route 21.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system crossed eastern New York on July 1, 2025. Warm and humid conditions resulted in heat index values (feels-like temperatures) reaching 95 to 100 degrees across the mid-Hudson Valley during the afternoon hours. In addition, showers and thunderstorms, some severe, developed ahead of a cold front downing trees and wires.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed with branches blocking Albany Post Road at Market Street in the Town of Hyde Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid-level shortwave and pre-frontal trough were able to fire up showers and thunderstorms in an area characterized by about 1500 J/kg of MLCAPE and 25-30 knots of effective shear. A few storms were able to become severe and produce localized damaging winds in southeast NY.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees down blocking right lane of I 87 Northbound.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another complex winter storm impacted eastern New York, this time just after Christmas, December 26-27, 2025. After days worth of storm track shifting in the guidance, the resulting more northerly track of the storm brought widespread moderate to heavy snow across most of the region. The heaviest snow fell within portions of the Eastern Catskills, immediate Capital District, Mid-Hudson Valley, and southern Taconics where snowfall totals ranged from about 5 to 10 inches. The Southern Adirondacks and Upper-Hudson Valley also received snow as a result of this event, though only light to locally moderate accumulations of about 2 to 6 inches were reported. ||The Governor of New York issued a state of emergency in Albany, Columbia, Dutchess, Fulton, Greene, Herkimer, Montgomery, Saratoga, Schenectady, Schoharie, Rensselaer, and Ulster Counties beginning with this event and spanning the lake effect and ice events that followed shortly thereafter due to the imminent danger the resulting conditions posed to public transportation, utility services, public health, and public safety systems. ||Key impacts: traffic accidents, travel delays, state of emergency declaration.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Zone average snowfall of 7.8 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sub-freezing temperatures ahead of an approaching warm front resulted in a period of accumulating freezing rain for all of eastern New York during the late afternoon and evening hours on December 28, 2025 into the overnight or morning hours on December 29, 2025. As temperatures rose above freezing, precipitation eventually changed to plain rain for many areas except across portions of the Upper Hudson Valley, Adirondacks, and Mohawk Valley, which remained at or below freezing until later Monday morning. ||Overall liquid equivalent precipitation amounts varied across the region with the greatest amounts (0.75 to 1.50 inches) occurring across areas north and west of the Capital Region. Sporadic flat ice accumulation reports estimated an approximate 30-40% ice accretion rate for many areas except 40-60% for areas north and west of the Capital Region, where the higher QPF amounts occurred. As a result, flat ice accumulations of a light glaze to 0.25 inches occurred for many areas except pockets of 0.40 to 0.75 inches across the Upper Hudson Valley, Adirondacks and Mohawk Valley. ||The accumulation of ice on trees and power lines led to isolated to numerous power outages with the greatest coverage of outages occurring across Herkimer, Hamilton, Fulton, Montgomery and Saratoga counties.||The Governor of New York issued a state of emergency in Albany, Columbia, Dutchess, Fulton, Greene, Herkimer, Montgomery, Saratoga, Schenectady, Schoharie, Rensselaer, and Ulster Counties on December 26th that extended through December 29th due to the imminent danger the resulting conditions of the active pattern over these days posed to public transportation, utility services, public health, and public safety systems. ||Key impacts: travel delays, power outages, state of emergency.

A slow-moving cold front triggered the development of scattered showers and thunderstorms across the area. An unstable environment with sufficient wind shear allows some storms to turn severe, producing damaging wind gusts and hail. One of the severe storms moved over the fairgrounds during the Great New York Fair leading to scattered damage on the grounds and one injury. Storms also led to the formation of a waterspout off of Lewis Point on Oneida Lake.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds resulted in multiple reports of trees and wires down north and west of Baldwinsville.

As temperatures rose above freezing, precipitation eventually changed to plain rain for many areas except across portions of the Upper Hudson Valley, Adirondacks, and Mohawk Valley, which remained at or below freezing until later Monday morning. ||Overall liquid equivalent precipitation amounts varied across the region with the greatest amounts (0.75 to 1.50 inches) occurring across areas north and west of the Capital Region. Sporadic flat ice accumulation reports estimated an approximate 30-40% ice accretion rate for many areas except 40-60% for areas north and west of the Capital Region, where the higher QPF amounts occurred. As a result, flat ice accumulations of a light glaze to 0.25 inches occurred for many areas except pockets of 0.40 to 0.75 inches across the Upper Hudson Valley, Adirondacks and Mohawk Valley. ||The accumulation of ice on trees and power lines led to isolated to numerous power outages with the greatest coverage of outages occurring across Herkimer, Hamilton, Fulton, Montgomery and Saratoga counties.||The Governor of New York issued a state of emergency in Albany, Columbia, Dutchess, Fulton, Greene, Herkimer, Montgomery, Saratoga, Schenectady, Schoharie, Rensselaer, and Ulster Counties on December 26th that extended through December 29th due to the imminent danger the resulting conditions of the active pattern over these days posed to public transportation, utility services, public health, and public safety systems. ||Key impacts: travel delays, power outages, state of emergency.

Although shear was overall weak, lift from an approaching pre-frontal trough and upper level trough combined with 1000-2000 J/kg of MLCAPE was enough for a few severe thunderstorms to develop and produce damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Utility company states many weather related power outages across Brooklyn, with 5000+ customers without power near Midwood.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Downed wires on Avenue J in Brooklyn.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Although shear was overall weak, lift from an approaching pre-frontal trough and upper level trough combined with 1000-2000 J/kg of MLCAPE was enough for a few severe thunderstorms to develop and produce damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning strike caused traffic light to catch fire at Avenue K and Kings Highway in  Brooklyn.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving cold front triggered the development of scattered showers and thunderstorms across the area. An unstable environment with sufficient wind shear allows some storms to turn severe, producing damaging wind gusts and hail. One of the severe storms moved over the fairgrounds during the Great New York Fair leading to scattered damage on the grounds and one injury. Storms also led to the formation of a waterspout off of Lewis Point on Oneida Lake.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds led to several barns being damaged and one destroyed along River Road. Multiple trees fell on houses and cars along Jordan, River and Southard Roads. Siding was torn off of one house and another had garage damage. Additional damage was noted on Grimes Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another complex winter storm impacted eastern New York, this time just after Christmas, December 26-27, 2025. After days worth of storm track shifting in the guidance, the resulting more northerly track of the storm brought widespread moderate to heavy snow across most of the region. The heaviest snow fell within portions of the Eastern Catskills, immediate Capital District, Mid-Hudson Valley, and southern Taconics where snowfall totals ranged from about 5 to 10 inches. The Southern Adirondacks and Upper-Hudson Valley also received snow as a result of this event, though only light to locally moderate accumulations of about 2 to 6 inches were reported. ||The Governor of New York issued a state of emergency in Albany, Columbia, Dutchess, Fulton, Greene, Herkimer, Montgomery, Saratoga, Schenectady, Schoharie, Rensselaer, and Ulster Counties beginning with this event and spanning the lake effect and ice events that followed shortly thereafter due to the imminent danger the resulting conditions posed to public transportation, utility services, public health, and public safety systems. ||Key impacts: traffic accidents, travel delays, state of emergency declaration.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Zone average snowfall of 6.1 inches.

There were dozens of instances of mostly minor damage from fair vendors, though some heavily damaged tents or awnings. Someone was injured when a rope-tethered plank of wood got caught by the wind and struck them.

Strong surface low in Canada between James Bay and the Great Lakes traversed Ontario and Quebec provinces on December 19th. This storm pushed a cold front across NY and VT during the morning hours with strong, gusty and damaging southwest-west winds in excess of 60 mph in several locations resulting in damaged trees, utility lines and isolated to scattered power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several wind gusts in excess of 55 mph with scattered tree damage and power outages.

This storm pushed a cold front across NY and VT during the morning hours with strong, gusty and damaging southwest-west winds in excess of 60 mph in several locations resulting in damaged trees, utility lines and isolated to scattered power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several wind gusts in excess of 60 mph with scattered tree damage and power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving cold front triggered the development of scattered showers and thunderstorms across the area. An unstable environment with sufficient wind shear allows some storms to turn severe, producing damaging wind gusts and hail. One of the severe storms moved over the fairgrounds during the Great New York Fair leading to scattered damage on the grounds and one injury. Storms also led to the formation of a waterspout off of Lewis Point on Oneida Lake.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds resulted in multiple reports of trees and wires down in the Cicero area, especially to the southeast.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms formed along and ahead of a stationary front draped from southwest Pennsylvania to the east end of Lake Ontario. These storms moved very slowly over the Southern Tier of New York with repeating heavy rain producing cells concentrated over Steuben County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several roadways were reported to be flooded in Painted Post.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another complex winter storm impacted eastern New York, this time just after Christmas, December 26-27, 2025. After days worth of storm track shifting in the guidance, the resulting more northerly track of the storm brought widespread moderate to heavy snow across most of the region. The heaviest snow fell within portions of the Eastern Catskills, immediate Capital District, Mid-Hudson Valley, and southern Taconics where snowfall totals ranged from about 5 to 10 inches. The Southern Adirondacks and Upper-Hudson Valley also received snow as a result of this event, though only light to locally moderate accumulations of about 2 to 6 inches were reported. ||The Governor of New York issued a state of emergency in Albany, Columbia, Dutchess, Fulton, Greene, Herkimer, Montgomery, Saratoga, Schenectady, Schoharie, Rensselaer, and Ulster Counties beginning with this event and spanning the lake effect and ice events that followed shortly thereafter due to the imminent danger the resulting conditions posed to public transportation, utility services, public health, and public safety systems. ||Key impacts: traffic accidents, travel delays, state of emergency declaration.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Zone average snowfall of 7.5 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sub-freezing temperatures ahead of an approaching warm front resulted in a period of accumulating freezing rain for all of eastern New York during the late afternoon and evening hours on December 28, 2025 into the overnight or morning hours on December 29, 2025. As temperatures rose above freezing, precipitation eventually changed to plain rain for many areas except across portions of the Upper Hudson Valley, Adirondacks, and Mohawk Valley, which remained at or below freezing until later Monday morning. ||Overall liquid equivalent precipitation amounts varied across the region with the greatest amounts (0.75 to 1.50 inches) occurring across areas north and west of the Capital Region. Sporadic flat ice accumulation reports estimated an approximate 30-40% ice accretion rate for many areas except 40-60% for areas north and west of the Capital Region, where the higher QPF amounts occurred. As a result, flat ice accumulations of a light glaze to 0.25 inches occurred for many areas except pockets of 0.40 to 0.75 inches across the Upper Hudson Valley, Adirondacks and Mohawk Valley. ||The accumulation of ice on trees and power lines led to isolated to numerous power outages with the greatest coverage of outages occurring across Herkimer, Hamilton, Fulton, Montgomery and Saratoga counties.||The Governor of New York issued a state of emergency in Albany, Columbia, Dutchess, Fulton, Greene, Herkimer, Montgomery, Saratoga, Schenectady, Schoharie, Rensselaer, and Ulster Counties on December 26th that extended through December 29th due to the imminent danger the resulting conditions of the active pattern over these days posed to public transportation, utility services, public health, and public safety systems. ||Key impacts: travel delays, power outages, state of emergency.

Low pressure moved across the St. Lawrence valley of both Canada and the United States on December 10th. Snowfall overspread northern New York during the morning hours and ended after midnight on December 11th. The heaviest of the snowfall was across St. Lawrence county where wraparound (defamation band) set up during the evening hours and lingered.||Snowfall totals ranged from 7 to 11 inches in St. Lawrence county, 4 to 6 inches in Franklin county, 3 to 5 inches in western Clinton and Essex counties with 2 to 3 inches in the Champlain Valley.||Wraparound, upslope snowfall continued in the higher terrain of the northern Adirondacks through Thursday night.||School closures and several vehicle accidents were the main impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals of 6 to 10 inches were reported across St. Lawrence county with 11 inches in South Colton, 10 inches in Heuvelton and Edwards with 8 inches in Massena and Russell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hot and humid conditions ahead of an upper level disturbance and surface boundary generated enough instability to drive the development of scattered thunderstorms across eastern New York on the afternoon of August 13, 2025.  A cluster of storms reached severe magnitudes, inflicting tree and wire damage in Schoharie, Albany, and Greene Counties that lead to several road closures and subsequent traffic delays. Some of the roadways impacted include Harpersfield Road, Blodgett Hill Road, and Route 81. Damage to a car was additionally reported due to downed wires near Route 143 and Waterboard Road near Coeymans Hollow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A local fire department reported several trees and wires down with wires on a car near Route 143 and Waterboard Road near Coeymans Hollow.

Hot and humid conditions ahead of an upper level disturbance and surface boundary generated enough instability to drive the development of scattered thunderstorms across eastern New York on the afternoon of August 13, 2025.  A cluster of storms reached severe magnitudes, inflicting tree and wire damage in Schoharie, Albany, and Greene Counties that lead to several road closures and subsequent traffic delays. Some of the roadways impacted include Harpersfield Road, Blodgett Hill Road, and Route 81. Damage to a car was additionally reported due to downed wires near Route 143 and Waterboard Road near Coeymans Hollow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A local fire department reported a tree down on house on Morehouse Road in the Town of Coeymans.

A slow moving cold front tracking east across the western and north central New York area on August 24th, caused strong to severe thunderstorms to develop. Severe thunderstorms resulted in wind damage and large hail for portions of the area east of the Genesee Valley.  Thunderstorms initially developed over the eastern end of Lake Erie during the morning hours and strengthened and expanded in coverage through the morning and into the afternoon as the cold front and thunderstorms pushed east. Increasing instability and shear ahead of the front, along with the forcing from the front caused the thunderstorms to reach severe limits.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees downed in Weedsport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very potent low pressure system brought widespread strong winds to much of eastern New York on Halloween (October 31), 2025. Widespread gusts of 30 to nearly 50 mph were common especially during the evening and late evening hours. Numerous reports of downed trees were reported to NWS Albany wherein traffic patterns were altered due to lane blockages, especially in Albany County. Some of the impacted roadways within Albany County include State Route 143 in Ravena, State Routes 145 and 81 in Preston Hollow, County Route 411 in Westerlo, State Farm Road and Wormer Road in Guilderland, and Cedar Drove Road in New Scotland. Unfortunately, one death (direct) and injury were caused from this event due to a tree falling on a car in Ulster County. The 77 year old female passenger was killed and the 79 year old driver was seriously injured.

A very potent low pressure system brought widespread strong winds to much of eastern New York on Halloween (October 31), 2025. Widespread gusts of 30 to nearly 50 mph were common especially during the evening and late evening hours. Numerous reports of downed trees were reported to NWS Albany wherein traffic patterns were altered due to lane blockages, especially in Albany County. Some of the impacted roadways within Albany County include State Route 143 in Ravena, State Routes 145 and 81 in Preston Hollow, County Route 411 in Westerlo, State Farm Road and Wormer Road in Guilderland, and Cedar Drove Road in New Scotland. Unfortunately, one death (direct) and injury were caused from this event due to a tree falling on a car in Ulster County. The 77 year old female passenger was killed and the 79 year old driver was seriously injured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the wake of the freezing rain/ice storm, a cold front crossed the region bringing a return to colder conditions as well as gusty winds from the afternoon hours on December 29 through December 30, 2025. Widespread wind gusts on the order of 35 to 50 mph were observed with a few localized gusts in excess of 50 mph including at the Hudson Valley Regional Airport and the New York State Mesonet near Tannersville. Along the leading edge of colder air, a localized snow squall passed through the City of Albany during the early evening hours on December 29. In addition, lake effect snow showers developed mainly within the Mohawk Valley bringing localized snowfall amounts of 6 to 10 inches in spots. Some of these snow bands extended into the Capital Region/Hudson Valley bringing amounts of 3 to 5 inches, especially across Saratoga and Schenectady counties.||Key impacts: travel delays.

A developing coastal low pressure off the North Carolina coast slowly moved northeast toward the 40 degree North/70 degree West benchmark on December 2nd. Light snow overspread northern NY around daybreak and exited by early to mid-afternoon with several inches of snow in northeast NY but more than 6 inches in the Capital District of NY.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 2 to 5 inches were observed.

A passing surface low and cold front brought rain and snow to the area starting on the morning of December 10th. Snow ahead of the cold front was mostly found for areas east of Lake Ontario. Behind the passing cold front, any remaining rain changed to snow as temperatures quickly cooled. Widespread snow transitioned to lake enhanced and then lake effect snow east and then southeast of both Lake Erie & Ontario. Winter Storm Warnings were in effect for areas east of Lake Ontario due to the combination of widespread snow and lake effect. Lake Effect Snow Warnings were issued for areas southeast of both Lake Erie & Lake Ontario where snow was from the lake response off of both lakes. Snowfall amounts of 8-12 inches of snow fell in most areas where warnings were in effect, with the hardest hit areas reaching upward of 20 inches.||Some of the notable snowfall amounts include: 20.5 in Kennedy, 14.7 in Franklinville, 13.8 at the Highmarket, 9.6 in Barnes Corners and 8.5 in Boston.

Advisory and some Warning level snowfall occurred across SE New York. An quasi arctic / polar boundary moved across the region as elongated weak low pressure formed along the boundary and moved along the boundary to the east northeast. The region was on the cold side of the boundary which resulted in accumulating snowfall region wide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters reported 8.0 inches at 2 SSE Plainedge and 6.2 inches in Bellmore. Broadcast media reported 6.1 inches in South Merrick. The public reported 7.0 inches at 1 NNW East Massapequa and 6.2 inches in West Hempstead and 5.8 inches at 1 S South Massapequa.

The region was on the cold side of the boundary which resulted in accumulating snowfall region wide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS Employees reported 6.7 inches in Stony Brook and 6.2 inches in Centereach.  A trained spotter reported 7.8 inches in Saint James. The public reported 7.0 inches in Smithtown, 6.5 inches at 1 S Nesconset, 6.5 inches in Deer Park, 6.3 inches at 1 SW Northport and 6.0 inches in Mount Sinai.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported 6.0 inches in Copiague, and an NWS Employee reported 6.1 inches in Sayville. The public reported 7.0 inches in Blue Point, 6.5 inches in East Islip, 7.0 inches in Islandia, and 7.3 inches in Bohemia. An NWS Employee reported 7.1 inches at 1 NNE Sayville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Advisory and some Warning level snowfall occurred across SE New York. An quasi arctic / polar boundary moved across the region as elongated weak low pressure formed along the boundary and moved along the boundary to the east northeast. The region was on the cold side of the boundary which resulted in accumulating snowfall region wide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A high end advisory event took place as amounts spatially and numerically averaged just below warning criteria. Trained spotters reported 3.7 inches in Middletown, 6.6 inches at 3 WNW Warwick, and 6.5 inches in Monroe. A Cocorahs observer reported 6.0 inches in Port Jervis. The public reported 5.9 inches at 2 SW Greenwood Lake, 5.1 inches at 2 E Highland Mills, and 4.3 inches at 2 SSW Stewart Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A progressive shortwave from the west overran a cold high pressure system resulting in a significant snowfall across the region. Warm advection aloft was the main driver of the snowfall for much of the event. Advisory to Warning level snow fell across Southeastern New York.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters reported 4.0 inches at 3 WNW Warwick and 6.0 inches in Monroe. Cocorahs observers reported 5.1 inches at 4 SSE Chester, 4.0 inches in Warwick, and 3.6 inches in Port Jervis, 5.5 inches at 3.3 W New Windsor, and 6.0 inches at Cornwall-on-the- Hudson. The public reported 5.7 inches 2 SSW Stewart Airport and 5.6 inches at 2 E Highland Mills. Amateur radio reported 5.0 inches at 2 NW Stewart Airport.  Amounts averaged just short of warning criteria both numerically and spatially.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A progressive shortwave from the west overran a cold high pressure system resulting in a significant snowfall across the region. Warm advection aloft was the main driver of the snowfall for much of the event. Advisory to Warning level snow fell across Southeastern New York.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cocorahs observers reported 5.0 inches in Stony Point, and 4.9 inches at 0.6 SE New Hempstead. Trained spotters reported 4.2 inches at both New City and 1 E Congers, and 4.0 inches in Nyack.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A progressive low pressure system developed in the SE states and moved northeast off the Mid Atlantic coast and intensified as it pulled away to the northeast. Temperatures were marginal across the interior as 2 to 4 inches of wet snow fell across portions of Orange County, mainly north and northwest portions of the county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fire Department and Rescue reported 3.9 inches at 2 SW Middletown. The general public reported 3.1 inches at 2 S Montgomery Airport, 2.2 inches at Pine Bush, 2.2 inches at 1 WSW Balmvile, 2.2 inches at 2 E Highland Mills. Trained spotters reported 2.5 inches at 1 WNW Monroe, 2.3 inches at 1 NNE Chester, 2.0 inches at 2 SSW Middletown, and 2.0 inches at 1 NNE Chester.  Only 1.1 inches was reported by the general public in Peekskill in Westchester County as amounts were less further SE.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the wake of the freezing rain/ice storm, a cold front crossed the region bringing a return to colder conditions as well as gusty winds from the afternoon hours on December 29 through December 30, 2025. Widespread wind gusts on the order of 35 to 50 mph were observed with a few localized gusts in excess of 50 mph including at the Hudson Valley Regional Airport and the New York State Mesonet near Tannersville. Along the leading edge of colder air, a localized snow squall passed through the City of Albany during the early evening hours on December 29. In addition, lake effect snow showers developed mainly within the Mohawk Valley bringing localized snowfall amounts of 6 to 10 inches in spots. Some of these snow bands extended into the Capital Region/Hudson Valley bringing amounts of 3 to 5 inches, especially across Saratoga and Schenectady counties.||Key impacts: travel delays.

A passing surface low and cold front brought rain and snow to the area starting on the morning of December 10th. Snow ahead of the cold front was mostly found for areas east of Lake Ontario. Behind the passing cold front, any remaining rain changed to snow as temperatures quickly cooled. Widespread snow transitioned to lake enhanced and then lake effect snow east and then southeast of both Lake Erie & Ontario. Winter Storm Warnings were in effect for areas east of Lake Ontario due to the combination of widespread snow and lake effect. Lake Effect Snow Warnings were issued for areas southeast of both Lake Erie & Lake Ontario where snow was from the lake response off of both lakes. Snowfall amounts of 8-12 inches of snow fell in most areas where warnings were in effect, with the hardest hit areas reaching upward of 20 inches.||Some of the notable snowfall amounts include: 20.5 in Kennedy, 14.7 in Franklinville, 13.8 at the Highmarket, 9.6 in Barnes Corners and 8.5 in Boston.

Advisory and some Warning level snowfall occurred across SE New York. An quasi arctic / polar boundary moved across the region as elongated weak low pressure formed along the boundary and moved along the boundary to the east northeast. The region was on the cold side of the boundary which resulted in accumulating snowfall region wide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cocorahs reported 8.3 inches at Center Moriches and the public reported 8.2 inches at 1 NNE Center Moriches. The public reported 8.0 inches at 1 NNW Remsenburg-Speonk, 5.8 inches at 3 ENE Sag Harbor. An NWS Employee reported 5.1 inches at 1 SE East Quogue.

A strong low pressure system tracking from the Upper Great Lakes into Quebec brought a period of rainfall and strong winds to eastern New York from the evening hours on December 18 to the early morning hours on December 20, 2025. Winds started out southerly from the evening hours December 18 into the morning hours on December 19 before shifting to the west during the afternoon and evening hours on December 19 behind the passage of a strong cold front. Widespread wind gusts on the order of 40 to 55 mph were observed both ahead and behind the cold front with a peak wind gust of 65 mph measured at the New York State Mesonet near Cold Brook.

A strong low pressure system tracking from the Upper Great Lakes into Quebec brought a period of rainfall and strong winds to eastern New York from the evening hours on December 18 to the early morning hours on December 20, 2025. Winds started out southerly from the evening hours December 18 into the morning hours on December 19 before shifting to the west during the afternoon and evening hours on December 19 behind the passage of a strong cold front. Widespread wind gusts on the order of 40 to 55 mph were observed both ahead and behind the cold front with a peak wind gust of 65 mph measured at the New York State Mesonet near Cold Brook.

Low pressure moving across the Ohio and Tennessee River Valleys and over the southern Appalachians transferred its energy to a surface low near the Carolina coast early on the 2nd. The coastal low strengthened as it moved northward just off the mid-Atlantic coast, drawing plenty of moisture north leading to a quick-hitting widespread snowfall during the morning and afternoon. As snow tapered off from west to east during the afternoon and early evening, it ended as a little freezing drizzle or sleet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated between 2 and 8 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moving across the Ohio and Tennessee River Valleys and over the southern Appalachians transferred its energy to a surface low near the Carolina coast early on the 2nd. The coastal low strengthened as it moved northward just off the mid-Atlantic coast, drawing plenty of moisture north leading to a quick-hitting widespread snowfall during the morning and afternoon. As snow tapered off from west to east during the afternoon and early evening, it ended as a little freezing drizzle or sleet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated between 3 and 6 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Flow became westerly and the atmosphere cooled in the wake of a cold front, leading to lake effect snow developing over north-central NY Saturday morning of the 21st before tapering off later Saturday afternoon. Snow bands downwind of Lake Ontario led to accumulations of 7 to 14 inches in northern areas of Oneida County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated between 7 and 14 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system passed north of the region on Monday, the 29th, with a trailing cold front transitioning winds to the northwest and advecting cold air over the lakes. This setup established a multi-lake connection that persisted through Wednesday, the 31st with snow becoming heavy at times. Snowfall rates reached 2 to 3 inches per hour within the most intense bands, resulting in accumulations of 9 to 20 inches across Onondaga, Madison, and Oneida counties. Strong winds with gusts in excess of 40 mph at times accompanied the system through Tuesday afternoon, the 30th. This caused significant blowing and drifting snow with localized whiteout conditions before the activity diminished on the 31st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated between 14 and 20 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure tracked across the central Great Lakes, into southern Ontario and then into southwest Quebec on December 29th. Its strong occluded front crossed the area during the morning into the early afternoon hours. Strong to damaging winds began to impact portions of the western and north central New York area as the front crosses the area and continued as cold air filtered into the region. Several reports were received of trees that were downed due to the strong winds and wind gusts of 60 to 79 mph were observed at area airports. ||The colder air and repeated passages of troughs and cold fronts for the remainder of the New Years week developed into a prolonged lake effect snow event, bringing at least light snow to the entire area. Moderate to heavy lake effect snow at times, combined with breezy conditions, prompted the issuance of Winter Storm Warnings for many counties in the Buffalo County Warning Area. Lake effect bands shifted north to south and south to north with passing troughs and cold fronts during the week, leaving a large footprint of significant snowfall. Snow and breezy conditions impacted New Years Eve celebrations across the areas as well. The hardest hit areas for snowfall were mainly east to southeast of both Lake Erie & Ontario. Some snow amounts from the entire event were as high as 4 to 5 feet off of Lake Erie and Lake Ontario.||Some notable wind gusts include: 79 mph at the Buffalo International Airport, 72 mph at the Skyway in Downtown Buffalo, and several other reports of wind gusts of 65 to 70 mph at western New York Mesonet sites. ||Some of the notable snowfall amounts from this event include: 59.0 inches in Lacona, 56.5 inches in Richland, 51.5 inches in Colden, 44.0 inches in Glenwood, 34.5 inches in Cattaraugus, 33.6 inches in Silver Creek, 31.0 inches in Highmarket, 29.2 inches in Arcade, 25.5 inches in Batavia and 20.5 inches in Copenhagen.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured gust of 66 MPH at KIAG ASOS.|Tree downed on Townline Road in North Tonawanda.|Portions of a roof and facade blown off a building on Center Street in Lewiston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following the passage of a cold front, northwesterly flow over Lake Ontario led to the development of lake effect snow over portions of central NY. With the support of deep moisture and strong lift, narrow bands of heavy snow were observed. There were multiple reports of 7 to 8 inches in Onondaga, Madison, and Southern Oneida County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated between 3 and 7 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex, relatively progressive winter storm impacted eastern New York in the preliminary days of December, bringing widespread moderate to heavy snow particularly to portions of the immediate Capital District, Eastern Catskills, Upper-Hudson Valley, Taconics, and Mohawk Valley. This was the most significant snowstorm this region had seen in several years with warning-level snow observed at Albany International Airport for the first time since 2023. ||An initial area of snow ahead of the main stratiform precipitation shield tracked through the region in the early morning hours of December 2, leading to a quick accumulation of a trace up to an inch in some isolated areas. The main precipitation shield then moved in around or just after 7 AM, bringing moderate to heavy snowfall rates in areas where enhanced forcing created localized, embedded mesoscale snow bands. Snow concluded late December 2/early December 3 with total accumulations ranging from about 2 to 12 inches. The heaviest snowfall accumulations fell in the aforementioned areas of the immediate Capital District, Eastern Catskills, Upper-Hudson Valley, Taconics, and Mohawk Valley where those mesoscale bands were most prevalent and terrain influences enhanced snowfall rates. ||Being that this was not only the most significant storm in several years but also an early-season, significant snowfall acting like the first snowfall of the season, numerous traffic accidents were reported across the region. Numerous schools also closed or were on delay for December 2. ||Key Impacts: Traffic accidents, travel delays, school closures, school delays.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Zone average snowfall of 4.9 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front passed through southeastern New York during the afternoon of December 19, 2025. The pressure gradient tightened in response, and produced high winds during the morning and early evening hours on this day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple high wind gusts were recorded at mesonet sites, including 63 mph at 1009 AM Local Standard Time at Stony Brook (measurement taken at 159 feet above ground), and  a measured wind gust of 62 mph at Eatons Neck Lighthouse, NY at 443 PM Local Standard Time at an elevation of 71 feet above ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A progressive shortwave from the west overran a cold high pressure system resulting in a significant snowfall across the region. Warm advection aloft was the main driver of the snowfall for much of the event. Advisory to Warning level snow fell across Southeastern New York.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cocorahs observers reported 4.1 inches at 0.5 SE Malverne and at Massapequa Park, 3.5 inches at 0.4 SE Seaford, and 4.0 inches in Bellmore. Amateur radio reported 4.1 inches in Massapequa. The public reported 5.0 inches in Farmingdale, and 3.5 inches at 1 WSW Plainview. A trained spotter reported 3.5 inches at 1 SW Elmont.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A progressive shortwave from the west overran a cold high pressure system resulting in a significant snowfall across the region. Warm advection aloft was the main driver of the snowfall for much of the event. Advisory to Warning level snow fell across Southeastern New York.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters reported 5.8 inches in Stony Brook and 4.5 inches at 1 SW Mount Sinai. Broadcast Media reported 4.5 inches at 1 ENE Commack. NWS Employees reported 5.1 inches at 1 NE Lake Ronkonkoma, 5.2 inches at 1 WSW Poquott, and 4.6 inches at 1 SW Sunken Meadow Park.  The public reported 5.2 inches at 2 S Commack, 5.0 inches at 1 SE East Northport, and 4.4 inches at 1 S Nesconset. Cocorahs reported 5.5 inches at 2.1 W Setauket-East Setauket. Overall, high end advisory criteria was reported average wise and spatially across the zone, falling just short of warning criteria.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A progressive shortwave from the west overran a cold high pressure system resulting in a significant snowfall across the region. Warm advection aloft was the main driver of the snowfall for much of the event. Advisory to Warning level snow fell across Southeastern New York.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An official NWS Observation from Islip was reported at 6.6 inches. The public reported 7.1 inches at 1 W Shirley Airport, 5.5 inches at Holbrook, 6.3 inches at 1 WNW East Patchogue, and 6.1 inches at 2 NNW Islip. Trained spotters reported 5.6 inches at 1 SSE Bohemia and 5.3 inches in Deer Park. NWS Employees reported 5.1 inches at 1 ESE Patchogue and 5.1 inches in Sayville. Cocorahs reported 4.4 inches in Amityville.  Overall the zone appeared to reach warning criteria spatially and with totals accumulated by midnight, some compaction occurred with most reports not reported until several hours after the accumulating snow had ended.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the wake of the freezing rain/ice storm, a cold front crossed the region bringing a return to colder conditions as well as gusty winds from the afternoon hours on December 29 through December 30, 2025. Widespread wind gusts on the order of 35 to 50 mph were observed with a few localized gusts in excess of 50 mph including at the Hudson Valley Regional Airport and the New York State Mesonet near Tannersville. Along the leading edge of colder air, a localized snow squall passed through the City of Albany during the early evening hours on December 29. In addition, lake effect snow showers developed mainly within the Mohawk Valley bringing localized snowfall amounts of 6 to 10 inches in spots. Some of these snow bands extended into the Capital Region/Hudson Valley bringing amounts of 3 to 5 inches, especially across Saratoga and Schenectady counties.||Key impacts: travel delays.

A strong low pressure system tracking from the Upper Great Lakes into Quebec brought a period of rainfall and strong winds to eastern New York from the evening hours on December 18 to the early morning hours on December 20, 2025. Winds started out southerly from the evening hours December 18 into the morning hours on December 19 before shifting to the west during the afternoon and evening hours on December 19 behind the passage of a strong cold front. Widespread wind gusts on the order of 40 to 55 mph were observed both ahead and behind the cold front with a peak wind gust of 65 mph measured at the New York State Mesonet near Cold Brook.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A two vehicle crash occurred during the early afternoon on the 4th. Snow was occurring during the time of the crash with embedded heavier bands nearby at the reported time of the incident, 1:43pm. The incident occurred on Route 12B in the town of Sherburne where a woman's vehicle and a tractor-trailer hit head-on. Law enforcement investigation revealed the tractor-trailer was driving north on 12B when a white-out snow squall passed through. The truck driver lost control and crossed into the southbound lane, colliding with the car unfortunately resulting in the death of the other driver.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A female driver passed away from a head-on collision with a tractor-trailer which was traveling northbound on Route 12B and lost control during a snow squall. The truck crossed into the southbound lane colliding with the other vehicle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A clipper system brought widespread precipitation to central NY. Several counties received 6 to 10 inches of snow as this system moved through. Warm air aloft led to an area of mixed precipitation and even freezing rain. Reports of ice accumulations ranged from a trace to 0.2 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated between 6 and 10 inches.

The main precipitation shield then moved in around or just after 7 AM, bringing moderate to heavy snowfall rates in areas where enhanced forcing created localized, embedded mesoscale snow bands. Snow concluded late December 2/early December 3 with total accumulations ranging from about 2 to 12 inches. The heaviest snowfall accumulations fell in the aforementioned areas of the immediate Capital District, Eastern Catskills, Upper-Hudson Valley, Taconics, and Mohawk Valley where those mesoscale bands were most prevalent and terrain influences enhanced snowfall rates. ||Being that this was not only the most significant storm in several years but also an early-season, significant snowfall acting like the first snowfall of the season, numerous traffic accidents were reported across the region. Numerous schools also closed or were on delay for December 2. ||Key Impacts: Traffic accidents, travel delays, school closures, school delays.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Zone average snowfall of 7.4 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front passed through southeastern New York during the afternoon of December 19, 2025. The pressure gradient tightened in response, and produced high winds during the morning and early evening hours on this day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported a large tree down in Port Jefferson near North Country Road and 2nd Avenue at approximately 1130 AM Local Standard Time. Additionally, the Department of Highways reported a downed tree near Commack Road along the Long Island Expressway North Service Road in Dix Hills, NY at approximately 1130 AM Local Standard Time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front passed through southeastern New York during the afternoon of December 19, 2025. The pressure gradient tightened in response, and produced high winds during the morning and early evening hours on this day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple high wind gusts were measured across the area, including 59 mph at ASOS station KHWV - Shirley Airport at 954 AM, 59 mph at mesonet station XMOR - East Moriches Campground at 1000 AM, 60 mph at mesonet station EW4378 - Brookhaven at 1000 AM, and 61 mph at mesonet station XSHN - Shinnecock in Hampton Bays at 1000 AM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front passed through southeastern New York during the afternoon of December 19, 2025. The pressure gradient tightened in response, and produced high winds during the morning and early evening hours on this day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were multiple reports of downed trees or limbs in the area, including a NWS Employee reporting a large downed tree branch on Montauk Highway in Remsenburg-Speonk at 1005 AM Local Standard Time, and a trained spotter reporting a large tree down on Montauk Highway in Bridgehampton at  1035 AM Local Standard Time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front passed through southeastern New York during the afternoon of December 19, 2025. The pressure gradient tightened in response, and produced high winds during the morning and early evening hours on this day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet station in Syosset recorded at 62 mph high wind gust at 924 AM Local Standard Time. Additionally, broadcast media reported live wires down on a car, setting it on fire along with an adjacent vehicle also catching fire on West Mill Drive in Great Neck at approximately 1015 AM Local Standard Time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front passed through southeastern New York during the afternoon of December 19, 2025. The pressure gradient tightened in response, and produced high winds during the morning and early evening hours on this day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 642 PM Local Standard Time, a high wind speed gust of 58 mph was recorded at mesonet station Robbins Reef Lighthouse in Bayonne, NJ, at an elevation of 69 feet above ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Developing and strengthening low pressure across the Great Lakes on December 28th moved across Ontario and Quebec provinces on December 29th. Mild, moist flow accompanying this system moved into cold, dense low level cold air entrenched across northern NY and VT.||Precipitation mainly fell as freezing rain during the evening hours of December 28th into the early morning hours of December 29th. This led to very hazardous driving conditions with numerous vehicle accidents as well as enough ice accretion on trees to cause some isolated power outages. Elevated flat ice accumulations across northern NY was generally 0.1 to 0.25 inches with amounts approaching 0.5 inches across the St. Lawrence valley as well as the deeper sheltered mountain valleys of western Essex county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Elevated flat ice accumulations were generally 0.1 to 0.3 inches across the area with vehicle accidents being the main impact.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A progressive shortwave from the west overran a cold high pressure system resulting in a significant snowfall across the region. Warm advection aloft was the main driver of the snowfall for much of the event. Advisory to Warning level snow fell across Southeastern New York.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported 7.5 inches in Orient, and 7.0 inches in Mattituck. A cooperative observer reported 6.2 inches in Baiting Hollow. An NWS Employee reported 6.1 inches at 3 WNW Riverhead.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A progressive shortwave from the west overran a cold high pressure system resulting in a significant snowfall across the region. Warm advection aloft was the main driver of the snowfall for much of the event. Advisory to Warning level snow fell across Southeastern New York.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported 6.3 inches at 1 NNE Manorville. The public reported 6.1 inches at 1 NNW Remsemburg-Speonk. Cocorahs reported 5.9 inches in Center Moriches and a cooperative observer reported 5.9 inches in Bridgehampton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front passed through southeastern New York during the afternoon of December 19, 2025. The pressure gradient tightened in response, and produced high winds during the morning and early evening hours on this day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple high wind gusts were recorded at mesonet sites, including 60 mph at station SOUT - Southold in Peconic at 1015 AM, 67 mph at Great Gull Island at 1048 AM, 64 mph in Orient at 1055 AM, and 59 mph at Fishers Island Airport. All times are reported in Local Standard Time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front passed through southeastern New York during the afternoon of December 19, 2025. The pressure gradient tightened in response, and produced high winds during the morning and early evening hours on this day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple high wind gusts were recorded at ASOS and mesonet sites, including 64 mph at mesonet station XHCK - Great South Bay in Amityville at 942 AM, 60 mph at ASOS station KISP McArthur Airport at 953 AM, 58 mph at mesonet station XBLU - Blue Point at 954 AM, 59 mph at mesonet station FW3143 - West Gilgo Beach at 955 AM, 62 mph at ASOS station KFRG - Farmingdale Airport at 956 AM, and 60 mph at the Brookhaven CWOP station at 1000 AM. Additionally, a trained spotter measured a gust of 66 mph in Farmingville at 1025 AM. All times are reported in Local Standard Time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered snow squalls developed ahead of and with an arctic cold front that tracked through eastern New York from west to east from late morning through the afternoon of December 4. Squalls were mostly confined to portions of the Mohawk Valley, Helderbergs and immediate Capital District where several New York State Mesonet and Department of Transportation highway cameras captured visibility at or under one quarter of a mile due to blowing snow from the combination of falling snow and gusty winds. Additionally, the Albany International Airport ASOS METAR reported blowing snow and one quarter mile visibility in the afternoon of December 4. Wind gusts captured by the ASOS and New York State Mesonet cameras ranged from about 30 to 45 mph. ||Key impacts: hazardous travel due to reduced visibility and slick roadways, travel delays.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow squall. NYS Mesonet at Herkimer shows a snow squall producing visibility below one quarter mild along with a measured wind gust of 33 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong clipper system impacted eastern New York on December 10, bringing moderate to heavy snow to portions of the Southern Adirondacks; Upper-Hudson Valley; and Mohawk Valley, and light to locally moderate snow to the rest of the region. The heaviest snow not only resulted from the adjacency to the best forcing from the storm, but localized terrain and lake enhancement. Within the Southern Adirondacks, Upper-Hudson Valley, and Mohawk Valley snow accumulations ranged largely from about 4 to 12 inches. Elsewhere, accumulations ranged from about 0.5 to 5 inches. ||Key impacts: traffic accidents, travel delays.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Zone average snowfall of 6.8 inches and liquid equivalent of 0.6 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Elevated flat ice accumulations were generally 0.2 to 0.4 inches across the area with vehicle accidents being the main impact, yet there were some isolated to scattered power outages as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex, relatively progressive winter storm impacted eastern New York in the preliminary days of December, bringing widespread moderate to heavy snow particularly to portions of the immediate Capital District, Eastern Catskills, Upper-Hudson Valley, Taconics, and Mohawk Valley. This was the most significant snowstorm this region had seen in several years with warning-level snow observed at Albany International Airport for the first time since 2023. ||An initial area of snow ahead of the main stratiform precipitation shield tracked through the region in the early morning hours of December 2, leading to a quick accumulation of a trace up to an inch in some isolated areas. The main precipitation shield then moved in around or just after 7 AM, bringing moderate to heavy snowfall rates in areas where enhanced forcing created localized, embedded mesoscale snow bands. Snow concluded late December 2/early December 3 with total accumulations ranging from about 2 to 12 inches. The heaviest snowfall accumulations fell in the aforementioned areas of the immediate Capital District, Eastern Catskills, Upper-Hudson Valley, Taconics, and Mohawk Valley where those mesoscale bands were most prevalent and terrain influences enhanced snowfall rates. ||Being that this was not only the most significant storm in several years but also an early-season, significant snowfall acting like the first snowfall of the season, numerous traffic accidents were reported across the region. Numerous schools also closed or were on delay for December 2. ||Key Impacts: Traffic accidents, travel delays, school closures, school delays.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Zone average snowfall of 5.8 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered snow squalls developed ahead of and with an arctic cold front that tracked through eastern New York from west to east from late morning through the afternoon of December 4. Squalls were mostly confined to portions of the Mohawk Valley, Helderbergs and immediate Capital District where several New York State Mesonet and Department of Transportation highway cameras captured visibility at or under one quarter of a mile due to blowing snow from the combination of falling snow and gusty winds. Additionally, the Albany International Airport ASOS METAR reported blowing snow and one quarter mile visibility in the afternoon of December 4. Wind gusts captured by the ASOS and New York State Mesonet cameras ranged from about 30 to 45 mph. ||Key impacts: hazardous travel due to reduced visibility and slick roadways, travel delays.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow squall. NYS Mesonet at Johnstown showed a snow squall producing visibility below one quarter of a mile along with a measured wind gust of 38 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong clipper system impacted eastern New York on December 10, bringing moderate to heavy snow to portions of the Southern Adirondacks; Upper-Hudson Valley; and Mohawk Valley, and light to locally moderate snow to the rest of the region. Elsewhere, accumulations ranged from about 0.5 to 5 inches. ||Key impacts: traffic accidents, travel delays.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Zone average snowfall of 6.2 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A short, but intense lake effect snow band rotated north into southwestern St. Lawrence county during the evening hours of December 31st and ended shortly before midnight. Snowfall rates in excess of 3-4 inches per hour occurred at times with snowfall totals of 6 to 12 inches mainly west of Route 11 and closer to Route 37.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Intense lake effect snow fell between the Route 11 and Route 37 corridor. Snowfall rates were 3 to 4 inches per hour at times with total snowfall of 6 to 12 inches. Some specific totals include; 13 inches in the western sections of Gouverneur, 10 inches in Morristown             and 7.5 inches in Heuvelton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Advisory and some Warning level snowfall occurred across SE New York. An quasi arctic / polar boundary moved across the region as elongated weak low pressure formed along the boundary and moved along the boundary to the east northeast. The region was on the cold side of the boundary which resulted in accumulating snowfall region wide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Official NWS Observation of 7.0 inches was reported in Jericho. A COOP observer reported 9.0 inches in Syosett. The general public reported 7.9 inches at 1 WSW Upper Brookville, 6.5 inches in Sea Cliff, and 6.4 inches at Glen Cove, and 6.1 inches in East Williston. A trained spotter reported 5.8 inches in Manhasset Hills.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters reported high end advisory snow criteria with 4.8 inches at 1 WSW Armonk, 4.6 inches at White Plains, and 4.1 inches at 2 NW Woodlawn. The public reported 4.0 inches at 2 ENE Peekskill. Cocorahs reported 3.9 inches in South Salem, 3.5 inches in Shrub Oak, and 5.0 inches at 2 NE Ossining and 2 E Ossining.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system tracking from the Upper Great Lakes into Quebec brought a period of rainfall and strong winds to eastern New York from the evening hours on December 18 to the early morning hours on December 20, 2025. Winds started out southerly from the evening hours December 18 into the morning hours on December 19 before shifting to the west during the afternoon and evening hours on December 19 behind the passage of a strong cold front. Widespread wind gusts on the order of 40 to 55 mph were observed both ahead and behind the cold front with a peak wind gust of 65 mph measured at the New York State Mesonet near Cold Brook.

A passing surface low and cold front brought rain and snow to the area starting on the morning of December 10th. Snow ahead of the cold front was mostly found for areas east of Lake Ontario. Behind the passing cold front, any remaining rain changed to snow as temperatures quickly cooled. Widespread snow transitioned to lake enhanced and then lake effect snow east and then southeast of both Lake Erie & Ontario. Winter Storm Warnings were in effect for areas east of Lake Ontario due to the combination of widespread snow and lake effect. Lake Effect Snow Warnings were issued for areas southeast of both Lake Erie & Lake Ontario where snow was from the lake response off of both lakes. Snowfall amounts of 8-12 inches of snow fell in most areas where warnings were in effect, with the hardest hit areas reaching upward of 20 inches.||Some of the notable snowfall amounts include: 20.5 in Kennedy, 14.7 in Franklinville, 13.8 at the Highmarket, 9.6 in Barnes Corners and 8.5 in Boston.

Low pressure moving across the Ohio and Tennessee River Valleys and over the southern Appalachians transferred its energy to a surface low near the Carolina coast early on the 2nd. The coastal low strengthened as it moved northward just off the mid-Atlantic coast, drawing plenty of moisture north leading to a quick-hitting widespread snowfall during the morning and afternoon. As snow tapered off from west to east during the afternoon and early evening, it ended as a little freezing drizzle or sleet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated between 4 and 7 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A clipper system brought widespread precipitation to central NY. Several counties received 6 to 10 inches of snow as this system moved through. Warm air aloft led to an area of mixed precipitation and even freezing rain. Reports of ice accumulations ranged from a trace to 0.2 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated between 6 and 12 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A clipper system moved across northern NY and brought accumulating snowfall to portions of central NY. Most locations reported light accumulations as warmer air moved in and allowed for rain to mix in. For Northern Oneida County, conditions stayed cold enough for significant snowfall accumulations as nearly a foot of snow was observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated between 2 and 11 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex, relatively progressive winter storm impacted eastern New York in the preliminary days of December, bringing widespread moderate to heavy snow particularly to portions of the immediate Capital District, Eastern Catskills, Upper-Hudson Valley, Taconics, and Mohawk Valley. This was the most significant snowstorm this region had seen in several years with warning-level snow observed at Albany International Airport for the first time since 2023. ||An initial area of snow ahead of the main stratiform precipitation shield tracked through the region in the early morning hours of December 2, leading to a quick accumulation of a trace up to an inch in some isolated areas. The main precipitation shield then moved in around or just after 7 AM, bringing moderate to heavy snowfall rates in areas where enhanced forcing created localized, embedded mesoscale snow bands. Snow concluded late December 2/early December 3 with total accumulations ranging from about 2 to 12 inches. The heaviest snowfall accumulations fell in the aforementioned areas of the immediate Capital District, Eastern Catskills, Upper-Hudson Valley, Taconics, and Mohawk Valley where those mesoscale bands were most prevalent and terrain influences enhanced snowfall rates. ||Being that this was not only the most significant storm in several years but also an early-season, significant snowfall acting like the first snowfall of the season, numerous traffic accidents were reported across the region. Numerous schools also closed or were on delay for December 2. ||Key Impacts: Traffic accidents, travel delays, school closures, school delays.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Zone average snowfall of 4.3 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across northern NY during the evening hours of November 26th, setting up a uniformed, cold fetch of west-southwest winds across Lake Ontario. A lake effect snow band developed off of Lake Ontario during the night of 11/26 and moved into the Adirondacks of SE St. Lawrence and southern Franklin counties shortly after midnight on the 27th.||The lake effect snow band primarily remained across the are through the 27th (Thanksgiving) before shifting south of the region late in the evening. Snowfall totals ranged from 3 to 7 inches across southern Franklin county with 5 to 9 inches across SE St. Lawrence county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals ranged from 5 to 9 inches across the area, including 9 inches in Edwards with 6 inches in Star Lake and Cranberry Lake.

High winds occurred early in the morning of November 6, 2025 over Southeastern New York after a cold front passed through and low pressure strengthened over New England, tightening the pressure gradient over the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple high wind gusts were recorded at mesonet sites, including 70 mph at 112 AM Local Standard Time at Stony Brook (measurement taken at 159 feet above ground), and 61 mph at 103 AM Local Standard Time at Eatons Neck Lighthouse at an elevation of 71 feet above ground.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 1217 AM Local Standard Time, a measured high wind gust of 60 mph was recorded at ASOS station KHPN, Westchester County Airport, located in White Plains, NY.

An area of low pressure across New England during the morning hours of November 16th moved into Newfoundland by November 17th and continued to exit the region. ||A counterclockwise, cold, unstable flow across the mountains of northern New York allowed for a orographic, upslope snowfall event that began in the Adirondacks around midday on November 16th and ended around midday on the 17th.||Snowfall was largely confined to the higher elevations and favored northwest upslope regions with 3 to 6 inches of snow but snowfall totals exceeding 8 inches were observed in the highest elevations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals exceeded 8 inches across the highest terrain with 3 to 6 inches along many mountain upslope communities.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold airmass and large trough over the region, combined with warm lake temperatures setup a favorable lake effect response off of both Lake Erie and Lake Ontario from the afternoon on November 10th through the early afternoon of November 11th. ||While the snowfall didn't result in a widespread significant snowfall, some reports for Oswego and Chautauqua counties were above warning criteria. Some of the notable amounts include: 11.3 inches in Central Square, 8.0 inches in Constantia, 8.0 inches in Jamestown and 7.2 inches in Kennedy.
Spells of wet weather, beginning in October, allowed for severe drought (D2) conditions to end across northern Washington County in eastern New York on November 4, 2025 per the U.S. Drought Monitor.

An upper level trough of low pressure pushed through the Great Lakes region beginning on Thursday, the 27th initially producing west-southwesterly winds off the eastern Great Lakes. This led to the development of lake effect snow showers downwind of Lake Ontario north of Oneida County, but lake effect snow off of Lake Erie brought accumulating snow over Steuben County where a Winter Weather Advisory was in effect. As the trough lifted to the north and east, the flow became more west-northwesterly and combined with an injection of colder air, a more significant lake effect snow event began to set up early on Friday, the 28th. The lake effect band off of Lake Ontario dropped south, impacting areas near and north of the I-90 corridor. Multi-lake connections helped to enhance not only this main band of snow that streamed over north-central NY, but also a secondary Lake Huron to Lake Ontario band that set up farther south. The heaviest snowfall was observed over Onondaga, Madison and Oneida Counties into the 29th, and this is where Lake Effect Snow Warnings were in effect with Winter Weather Advisories for the adjacent counties. Lake effect snow showers continued into Saturday, then as the upper low drifted farther to the north, the flow became more southerly bringing an end to the lake effect. Strong winds accompanied this system with gusts upward of 40 to 50 mph recorded.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake effect snow accumulated between 5 and 8 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down on U.S. Route 9 at Clove Road near Fishkill per the Department of Highways.

The band then eventually drifted farther south and weakened overnight into the early morning hours on November 29.||Total snowfall accumulations of 7 to 15 inches were observed across Herkimer, Fulton and Montgomery counties. These bands even brought accumulations of 3 to 6 inches across portions of the Capital District as well as in Hamilton County. The heavy snow resulted in a portion of Route 28 in southern Herkimer County to close due to the whiteout conditions.||Key impacts: transportation delays, road closures.

A weak, but complex area of low pressure traveled from the Mid-Atlantic Appalachians on November 9th across eastern New England during the afternoon and evening of November 10th. ||Precipitation started as a cold rain (temperatures in the 30s to around 40) during the 10th. As low pressure tracked across eastern New England and slightly strengthened during the late afternoon and evening rain changed to snow, heavy at times during the evening commute. This accounted for numerous (dozens and dozens) vehicle accidents across the Champlain Valley. Highest snowfall totals were 4 to 7 inches in the Champlain Valley of Clinton and Essex counties, quickly dropping off to 2 to 4 inches elsewhere.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall accumulations of 4 to 7 inches were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High winds occurred in the afternoon of November 11, 2025 over Southeastern New York as the pressure gradient tightened over the region in response to high pressure building in from the southwest while an area of low pressure was strengthening near northern New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A measured high wind gust of 61 mph at 238 PM Local Standard Time at Eatons Neck Lighthouse at an elevation of 71 feet above ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the evening of November 3rd, a cold front crossed the region and was then followed by an area of showers over Lake Ontario that tracked southeast into Monroe County. The showers then strengthened to severe thunderstorms as they encountered stronger shear. The thunderstorms caused 1.00 inch hail in Monroe County and caused some wind damage in parts of Ontario County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Time estimated by radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure tracked across the central Great Lakes, into southern Ontario and then into southwest Quebec on December 29th. Its strong occluded front crossed the area during the morning into the early afternoon hours. Strong to damaging winds began to impact portions of the western and north central New York area as the front crosses the area and continued as cold air filtered into the region. Several reports were received of trees that were downed due to the strong winds and wind gusts of 60 to 79 mph were observed at area airports. ||The colder air and repeated passages of troughs and cold fronts for the remainder of the New Years week developed into a prolonged lake effect snow event, bringing at least light snow to the entire area. Moderate to heavy lake effect snow at times, combined with breezy conditions, prompted the issuance of Winter Storm Warnings for many counties in the Buffalo County Warning Area. Lake effect bands shifted north to south and south to north with passing troughs and cold fronts during the week, leaving a large footprint of significant snowfall. Snow and breezy conditions impacted New Years Eve celebrations across the areas as well. The hardest hit areas for snowfall were mainly east to southeast of both Lake Erie & Ontario. Some snow amounts from the entire event were as high as 4 to 5 feet off of Lake Erie and Lake Ontario.||Some notable wind gusts include: 79 mph at the Buffalo International Airport, 72 mph at the Skyway in Downtown Buffalo, and several other reports of wind gusts of 65 to 70 mph at western New York Mesonet sites. ||Some of the notable snowfall amounts from this event include: 59.0 inches in Lacona, 56.5 inches in Richland, 51.5 inches in Colden, 44.0 inches in Glenwood, 34.5 inches in Cattaraugus, 33.6 inches in Silver Creek, 31.0 inches in Highmarket, 29.2 inches in Arcade, 25.5 inches in Batavia and 20.5 inches in Copenhagen.

A frontal boundary stalled to the south of the ALY County Warning Area, in tandem with several mid- and upper-level disturbances generated widespread heavy rainfall and scattered thunderstorms across eastern New York on June 6 and 7. Prolonged, moderate to locally heavy downpours lead to widespread poor drainage and urban flooding with numerous roadways and residential roadways becoming inundated with standing water. Over the course of the two days, rainfall totals amounted to anywhere from an inch to three inches with isolated amounts of three and a half to nearly four inches in the Capital District. On June 6, one thunderstorm reached severe magnitude in eastern Duchess County, producing isolated tree damage on Deep Hollow Road.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A local 911 call center operator reported portions of Township Road in Knox being barricaded off due to flooding but stated that the road remained passable.

A cold front approached northern New York, specifically the  St. Lawrence county region during the afternoon of September 4th into a marginally unstable airmass. A broken line of showers and thunderstorms moved across St. Lawrence county with two storms producing marginal severe weather in the form of storing wind gusts and downed trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree and numerous branches downed by thunderstorm winds along NY Route 812.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On September 4, a pre-frontal trough and cold front crossing the region caused severe thunderstorms to develop across the eastern half of the Buffalo forecast area. With 40+ knots of 0-6 km shear available and CAPE values of around 1,000 J/kg for areas east of the Genesee Valley. As showers along the trough and front encountered this more favorable area and thunderstorms developed and became severe with damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few reports of trees downed in the Town of  Geneva.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moving across the area triggered scattered showers and thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening. With plenty of wind shear in place, the environment was favorable enough for a few storms embedded within a line to produce strong, damaging wind gusts across north-central New York. It was determined that an EF0 tornado had spun up near Ava in Northern Oneida County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm moved through far Northern Oneida County that had broad rotation tighten up as it approached Ava. A weak tornado touched down and traveled about 3 miles and was on the ground for approximately 8 minutes. The first area of damage that was surveyed was off of Vary Road west of Ava with a few trees down as well as scattered tree debris that fell in a convergent pattern. The next stop was in the town of Ava where there was damage that indicated a maximum of 80 mph winds. A chicken coop was lofted and set down several yards from its original location as well as a large tree that fell on a pig pen. Once again trees on the south side of the property fell in a convergent pattern. The last location that had any damage was at a farm house a |little over a mile to the east of Ava on Flint Town Road where a large, partly rotten tree fell on the house. Minor damage occurred to the property from the tree falling on it but no visible wind damage was seen. With the tree being weak and no other significant tree damage in the vicinity and no more visible damage to the east, this was determined to be roughly the end of the tornado's path.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary stalled to the south of the ALY County Warning Area, in tandem with several mid- and upper-level disturbances generated widespread heavy rainfall and scattered thunderstorms across eastern New York on June 6 and 7. Prolonged, moderate to locally heavy downpours lead to widespread poor drainage and urban flooding with numerous roadways and residential roadways becoming inundated with standing water. Over the course of the two days, rainfall totals amounted to anywhere from an inch to three inches with isolated amounts of three and a half to nearly four inches in the Capital District. On June 6, one thunderstorm reached severe magnitude in eastern Duchess County, producing isolated tree damage on Deep Hollow Road.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public reported that Central Avenue was flooded under the railroad  bridge by Yardboro Avenue. Police blocked off that section of the road and a car is stuck in the flood waters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure tracked across the central Great Lakes, into southern Ontario and then into southwest Quebec on December 29th. Its strong occluded front crossed the area during the morning into the early afternoon hours. Strong to damaging winds began to impact portions of the western and north central New York area as the front crosses the area and continued as cold air filtered into the region. Several reports were received of trees that were downed due to the strong winds and wind gusts of 60 to 79 mph were observed at area airports. ||The colder air and repeated passages of troughs and cold fronts for the remainder of the New Years week developed into a prolonged lake effect snow event, bringing at least light snow to the entire area. Moderate to heavy lake effect snow at times, combined with breezy conditions, prompted the issuance of Winter Storm Warnings for many counties in the Buffalo County Warning Area. Lake effect bands shifted north to south and south to north with passing troughs and cold fronts during the week, leaving a large footprint of significant snowfall. Snow and breezy conditions impacted New Years Eve celebrations across the areas as well. The hardest hit areas for snowfall were mainly east to southeast of both Lake Erie & Ontario. Some snow amounts from the entire event were as high as 4 to 5 feet off of Lake Erie and Lake Ontario.||Some notable wind gusts include: 79 mph at the Buffalo International Airport, 72 mph at the Skyway in Downtown Buffalo, and several other reports of wind gusts of 65 to 70 mph at western New York Mesonet sites. ||Some of the notable snowfall amounts from this event include: 59.0 inches in Lacona, 56.5 inches in Richland, 51.5 inches in Colden, 44.0 inches in Glenwood, 34.5 inches in Cattaraugus, 33.6 inches in Silver Creek, 31.0 inches in Highmarket, 29.2 inches in Arcade, 25.5 inches in Batavia and 20.5 inches in Copenhagen.

An upper level trough approached from the west along with an associated surface cold front. The environment over the area ahead of the front was able to destabilize with MLCAPE values reaching about 2000 J/kg. This, combined with ample Effective Bulk Shear of about 45 kt, allowed severe thunderstorms to develop as the cold front moved through the area. These storms produced damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires down on a vehicle on Hardscrabble Road and Reed Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted northward across Central New York early in the day which drew up plenty of moisture from the south ahead of an approaching cold front. This led to widespread showers, and enough instability was in place being in the warm sector that isolated thunderstorms developed. While instability was modest, strong wind shear was in place which allowed for a couple of storms to become strong to severe with damaging wind gusts. One storm along the warm front began to rotate and wound up producing a weak EF0 tornado near Callicoon Center in Sullivan County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Just before noon, a thunderstorm along a warm front separated from the rest of a line of showers and storms and began to rotate. With a favorable environment but low instability, a weak tornado touched down near Callicoon Center. With near peak foliage in parts of the Catskills, it was easy for the weak circulation to loft leaves into the storm that were detected by radar with a debris signature. The circulation was strongest on hilltops and weak to non existent in valleys.||The survey began near Callicoon Center where there was a house with several small trees that fell on the residence. Damage to the residence was minimal. Up the hill on J Young Road there was a greater amount of damage observed, with the tornado crossing paths with old tornado damage from April 22, 2023. There were larger trees that fell in a convergent pattern, and the widest section of the tornado was in this area, estimated to be about 150 yards wide. No damage was observed on Huber Rd and down towards Hazel, so its likely the circulation became disorganized and lifted. |Crossing Route 17 and going up Beaver Hill Rd, there was more damage along the path of rotation that had a cyclonic pattern with the trees that fell on each side of the road in line with  previous damage. No other damage was observed on roads that crossed the path of rotation to the town of Margaretville so the tornado was determined to have dissipated near the town of Lewbeach near the Sullivan county and Ulster County border.

Lake effect snow developed southeast of Lake Ontario early on December 12th as a shortwave trough crossed the region. The lake effect weakened briefly on the night of the 12th, but then re-developed on the morning of the 13th ahead of and then behind a strong cold front crossing the area. The heaviest lake effect snow focused mainly on areas east to southeast of both Lake Erie & Ontario, but ahead of the passing cold front with a pre-frontal trough crossing the area, lake snow initially developed northeast of both lakes. Snowfall amounts up to around 15 inches were measured east of Lake Ontario. Snowfall off of Lake Erie was mostly in the 10 to 14 inch range, however a report from Perrysburg of nearly 2 feet was received, where higher terrain and proximity to the lake made for favorable location of heavier snowfall.
