A Mesoscale Convective System (MCS) developed in Ontario and tracked south into New York in the early morning hours on Sunday, June 22. This system eventually tracked through the Western Adirondacks into the western Mohawk Valley, inflicting fairly widespread wind damage across southern Herkimer County. Numerous reports were relayed to NWS Albany of tree and wire damage that ultimately led to the blockage and closure of numerous roadways. Trees were also reported down on sheds, fences, and even on a mobile home (in the town of Schuyler) but no injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 911 call center operator reported trees and wires down blocking Cosby Manor Road in the town of Schuyler.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Mesoscale Convective System (MCS) developed in Ontario and tracked south into New York in the early morning hours on Sunday, June 22. This system eventually tracked through the Western Adirondacks into the western Mohawk Valley, inflicting fairly widespread wind damage across southern Herkimer County. Numerous reports were relayed to NWS Albany of tree and wire damage that ultimately led to the blockage and closure of numerous roadways. Trees were also reported down on sheds, fences, and even on a mobile home (in the town of Schuyler) but no injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A local emergency manager reported a tree down blocking one lane on NYS Route 5 and Elmwood Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure tracked across the central Great Lakes, into southern Ontario and then into southwest Quebec on December 29th. Its strong occluded front crossed the area during the morning into the early afternoon hours. Strong to damaging winds began to impact portions of the western and north central New York area as the front crosses the area and continued as cold air filtered into the region. Several reports were received of trees that were downed due to the strong winds and wind gusts of 60 to 79 mph were observed at area airports. ||The colder air and repeated passages of troughs and cold fronts for the remainder of the New Years week developed into a prolonged lake effect snow event, bringing at least light snow to the entire area. Moderate to heavy lake effect snow at times, combined with breezy conditions, prompted the issuance of Winter Storm Warnings for many counties in the Buffalo County Warning Area. Lake effect bands shifted north to south and south to north with passing troughs and cold fronts during the week, leaving a large footprint of significant snowfall. Snow and breezy conditions impacted New Years Eve celebrations across the areas as well. The hardest hit areas for snowfall were mainly east to southeast of both Lake Erie & Ontario. Some snow amounts from the entire event were as high as 4 to 5 feet off of Lake Erie and Lake Ontario.||Some notable wind gusts include: 79 mph at the Buffalo International Airport, 72 mph at the Skyway in Downtown Buffalo, and several other reports of wind gusts of 65 to 70 mph at western New York Mesonet sites. ||Some of the notable snowfall amounts from this event include: 59.0 inches in Lacona, 56.5 inches in Richland, 51.5 inches in Colden, 44.0 inches in Glenwood, 34.5 inches in Cattaraugus, 33.6 inches in Silver Creek, 31.0 inches in Highmarket, 29.2 inches in Arcade, 25.5 inches in Batavia and 20.5 inches in Copenhagen.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Warsaw Mesonet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure crossed the region on December 26th, bringing a wintry mix of rain, snow and freezing rain for areas of western New York. But areas farther east, where colder air was in place, mostly all snow fell. Snow was moderate to heavy at times for portions of Wayne, Northern Cayuga and Oswego counties during the afternoon and evening on December 26th, where snowfall amounts of 7 to 12 were received. The timing of the mixed precipitation and snow, caused some travel issues for the busy travel day, the day after Christmas. ||Some of the notable snowfall amounts include: 12.0 inches in Parish, 9.0 inches in Cato and 7.0 inches in Wolcott.

Strong winds developed on December 18th and continued into the 19th as a strong area of pressure passed north of the region. Winds initially were strongest for the favorable downslope areas before shifting out of the southwest and west behind a strong cold front crossing the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 68 MPH gust was also measured at Fort Drum (KGTB).
